Ayahs

الٓر ۚ تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَقُرْءَانٍ مُّبِينٍ (١)
Alif, Lam, Ra. These are the signs of the Book and a manifest Quran. (1)
رُّبَمَا يَوَدُّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ مُسْلِمِينَ (٢)
Much will the faithless wish that they had been Muslims. (2)
ذَرْهُمْ يَأْكُلُوا۟ وَيَتَمَتَّعُوا۟ وَيُلْهِهِمُ ٱلْأَمَلُ ۖ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ (٣)
Leave them to eat and enjoy and to be diverted by longings. Soon they will know. (3)
وَمَآ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا وَلَهَا كِتَابٌ مَّعْلُومٌ (٤)
We did not destroy any town but that it had a known term. (4)
مَّا تَسْبِقُ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلَهَا وَمَا يَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ (٥)
No nation can advance its time nor can it defer it. (5)

Content of Surah Al-Hijr

Surah Al-Hijr, according to the consensus among commentators, is a Meccan Surah. Based on the report from Ibn al-Nadim's Fihrist regarding the history of the Qur'an, it is the fifty-second Surah revealed to the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in the land of Mecca. All commentators agree that it consists of 99 verses.

For this reason, the same rhythm and tone of Meccan Surahs are fully reflected in it. As we have previously mentioned, Meccan Surahs primarily emphasize several themes: Islamic teachings—especially Monotheism (Tawhid) and the Resurrection (Ma'ad)—warning polytheists, sinners, and oppressors, and drawing lessons from the history of past nations.

Therefore, the content of this Surah can be summarized into seven sections:

  1. Verses related to the Origin of the universe and faith in Him through the study of the secrets of creation.
  2. Verses related to the Resurrection and the punishment of evildoers.
  3. The importance of the Qur'an and the greatness of this Heavenly Book.
  4. The story of the creation of Adam, the rebellion of Iblis, and his ultimate fate, serving as a warning and a wake-up call for all human beings!
  5. References to the history of peoples such as the people of Lot, Salih, and Shu'ayb, to complete this warning.
  6. Warnings, glad tidings, effective exhortations, crushing threats, and encouraging incentives.
  7. An invitation to the Prophet to remain steadfast and offering him comfort in the face of the intense conspiracies of the opponents, which were particularly numerous and dangerous in the environment of Mecca.

The name of this Surah is taken from the eightieth verse, which speaks about the "People of Al-Hijr" (the people of Salih). This is because there are five verses in this Surah regarding the People of Al-Hijr, and it is the only Surah that refers to the people of Salih by this title. The details of this will come, God willing, in the commentary of verses 80 to 84.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ الر تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ وَ قُرْآنٍ مُبِينٍ (1) رُبَما يَوَدُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ كانُوا مُسْلِمِينَ (2) ذَرْهُمْ يَأْكُلُوا وَ يَتَمَتَّعُوا وَ يُلْهِهِمُ الْأَمَلُ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ (3) وَ ما أَهْلَكْنا مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ إِلاَّ وَ لَها كِتابٌ مَعْلُومٌ (4) ما تَسْبِقُ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلَها وَ ما يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ (5)

Commentary

Vain Desires!

Once again, at the beginning of this Surah, we encounter the "Disjointed Letters" (Muqatta'at): Alif, Lam, Ra. These signify that this great Heavenly Book, which paves the way for all humans toward prosperity, is composed of simple alphabet letters—the same raw material available to all human beings, even two or three-year-old children. It is the ultimate degree of miracle that such an unparalleled product is fashioned from such materials.

Therefore, immediately following them, it says: "These are the verses of the Book and a clear Qur'an" (تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ وَ قُرْآنٍ مُبِينٍ).

We know that tilka is a demonstrative pronoun for something distant, whereas normally hadhihi (a pronoun for something near) should be used here. However, as we have stated before, in Arabic literature (and even in Persian), a distant demonstrative is sometimes used to express the greatness of something. That is, it possesses such grandeur that it is as if it is situated in the heavens at a far distance from us. This is exactly like when we say in the presence of a great person, "If that leader permits, we shall take such action." The word "that" here is to express the greatness of his status, just as the mention of "Qur'an" in an indefinite form (nakirah) is also to express its greatness.

In any case, the mention of "Qur'an" after "Book" is effectively for emphasis, and describing it as mubin (clear) is because it explains realities and clarifies truth from falsehood.

The possibility suggested by some commentators that "Book" here refers to the Torah and the Gospel seems very unlikely.

Then, He warns those who persist in stubbornness and opposition to these clear divine verses that a day will come when they will regret the bitter consequences of their disbelief, blind prejudice, and obstinacy: "Perhaps those who disbelieve will wish that they had been Muslims" (رُبَما يَوَدُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ كانُوا مُسْلِمِينَ).

Therefore, the meaning of yawaddu (they wish/love)—as explained in Tafsir al-Mizan—is "wishing" in the sense of longing (tamanni). The mention of the word law (if only) is evidence that they will wish for Islam at a time when they no longer have the power to return to it. This itself serves as an indication that this longing and wish will occur in the other world after witnessing the results of their deeds.

A Hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام in this regard also fully confirms this meaning: "On the Day of Resurrection, a caller will cry out such that all creatures hear: 'Today, none shall enter Paradise except those who were Muslims.' At that moment, the rest of the creatures will wish they had been Muslims." [^1]

It is also narrated from the Great Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that when the dwellers of Hell gather and a group of sinful Muslims are placed with them, the disbelievers will say to the Muslims: "Were you not Muslims?" They will reply: "Yes, we were." The disbelievers will respond: "Then your Islam did not benefit you, since

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verse discussed; the first Hadith is also narrated in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn from Tafsir al-Ayyashi, and Fakhr al-Razi in his commentary narrated a Hadith similar to the second one (with slight differences).

you are in the same place as us!"

The Muslims will say: "We had (great) sins for which we were afflicted with this fate." This confession of sin and fault, along with the taunting of the enemy, causes Allah to command: "Take out every believer and Muslim who is in Hell." At that moment, the disbelievers will say: "If only we too had been Muslims!" [^1]

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the verse that among the disbelievers are individuals who still have a spark of an awake conscience in their hearts. When they see the call of the Prophet of Islam and these verses of the Clear Book with their pleasing content, they become attracted to it in the depths of their hearts and wish they were Muslims. However, prejudices, stubbornness, or material interests do not allow them to accept this great reality, and thus they remain imprisoned in the dungeon of disbelief and lack of faith.

One of our faithful and mujahid friends who had traveled to Europe said: "When I was enumerating the merits of Islam to a Christian, who was a fair person, he replied to me: 'I truly congratulate you for following such a religion, but what can we do? Our life conditions do not allow us to abandon our faith!'"

Interestingly, we read in some Islamic narrations that when the messenger of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله brought his letter to Caesar of Rome, he privately expressed faith to the Prophet's messenger and even desired to call the Romans to the creed of Monotheism. However, he thought he should test them first. As soon as his soldiers sensed

[^1]: In Tafsir al-Tabari, several Hadiths with the same theme are narrated under this verse.

that he wanted to leave the Christian faith, they besieged his palace. He immediately declared to them: "My intention was only to test you; return to your places." Then he said to the Prophet's messenger: "I know that your Prophet is from Allah, and he is the one we were expecting, but what can I do? I fear that my government will be lost and my life is in danger." [^1]

In any case, it should be noted that these two interpretations are not in conflict. It is possible that the verse refers to the regret of groups of disbelievers both in that world and this world, while in neither place do they have the power to return—for different reasons! (Reflect on this).

Then, in a very crushing tone, He says: O Prophet! "Leave them to eat and enjoy themselves (like cattle), and let [false] hope divert them, for they will soon know" (ذَرْهُمْ يَأْكُلُوا وَ يَتَمَتَّعُوا وَ يُلْهِهِمُ الْأَمَلُ فَسوفَ يَعْلَمُونَ).

These people are like animals that understand nothing but the stable and fodder, and nothing but material pleasure; every movement they make is to attain these things.

Veils of arrogance, negligence, and long-term desires have so fallen upon their hearts and occupied them that they no longer have the capacity to perceive reality.

But when the slap of death strikes their faces, and the veils of negligence and arrogance are removed from before their eyes, and they find themselves on the threshold of death or in the arena of Resurrection—yes, then they will understand how negligent they were and to what extent they were losers

[^1]: Makatib al-Rasul, Vol. 1, p. 112.

and wretched, and how they lost the most precious of capitals for nothing!

In the next verse, so they do not imagine that this respite and enjoyment of worldly pleasures is endless, He adds: "And We did not destroy any city but that it had a known decree and an unchangeable time" (وَ ما أَهْلَكْنا مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا وَ لَها كِتابٌ مَعْلُومٌ).

And "No nation can precede its term, nor can they delay it" (ما تَسْبِقُ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلَها وَ ما يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ).

The Divine Sunnah (way) has always been to give sufficient respite for reconsideration, awakening, and awareness. He sends painful events and means of mercy one after another; He threatens, He encourages, and He warns so that the proof is completed for everyone.

But when this respite ends, the definite destiny will overtake them. Delay or haste may occur for educational interests, but as the saying goes, there is no escaping it! Is not attention to this reality enough for everyone to take a lesson from the fate of those who came before, and to use the period of Divine respite for return and reform? Must we sit and wait for the grim fate of previous misguided and oppressive nations to be repeated for us, and instead of taking lessons from our predecessors, become a lesson for those who come after us?

Furthermore, from these last two verses, the philosophy of "repeatedly stating the history of past peoples" in the verses of the Qur'an—even in this very Surah we are discussing—becomes clear.

Key Point: Long Desires as a Major Factor of Negligence

Undoubtedly, hope and desire—or in Arabic terms, amal—is the factor that moves the wheels of human life. If it were removed from the hearts of the people of the world for even a single day, the system of life would collapse, and few would find a reason for their activity, effort, and dynamism.

The famous Hadith narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Hope is a mercy for my nation; were it not for hope, no mother would nurse her child, and no planter would plant a tree," [^1] refers to this very reality.

However, this very factor of life and movement, if it exceeds its limit and takes the form of "long-term desires," becomes the worst factor of deviation and misery. It is exactly like rainwater, which is the source of life, but if it exceeds its limit, it becomes the cause of drowning and destruction.

This lethal desire is what the above verses emphasize, considering it the cause of being heedless of Allah, truth, and reality. These long-term desires and hopes so occupy a person and drown them in a world of imagination that they become completely alienated from life and its ultimate goal.

The famous Hadith narrated in Nahj al-Balagha from Ali عليه السلام is also an eloquent statement of this reality: "O people! The most fearful things I fear for you are two: following whims and long desires. As for following whims, it turns one away from the truth; and as for long desires, it makes one forget the Hereafter." [^1]

Truly, how many talented, worthy, and capable individuals, due to being trapped in the snare of long desires, turned into weak and distorted beings who not only failed to be useful to their society but also trampled their own personal interests and were hindered from any perfection. As we read in the Supplication of Kumayl: "And my long desires imprisoned me from my [true] benefit."

Fundamentally, when desire exceeds its limit, it constantly keeps a person in suffering and toil. Day and night they must strive, thinking they are pursuing happiness and comfort, while they provide nothing for themselves but misery and wretchedness. Such individuals often die in this state, and their painful and tragic lives serve as a lesson for those who have seeing eyes and hearing ears.

[^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 30 (root a-m-l). [^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 42.

Ayahs

وَقَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِى نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ ٱلذِّكْرُ إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ (٦)
They said, ‘O you, to whom the Reminder has been sent down, you are indeed crazy. (6)
لَّوْ مَا تَأْتِينَا بِٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٧)
Why do you not bring us the angels should you be truthful?!’ (7)
مَا نُنَزِّلُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَمَا كَانُوٓا۟ إِذًا مُّنظَرِينَ (٨)
We do not send down the angels except with due reason, and then they will not be granted any respite. (8)

Commentary

The Demand for the Descent of Angels

In these verses, the Quran first points to the hostile stances of the disbelievers against the Quran and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, stating: "They said: 'O you to whom the Reminder (the Quran) has been sent down, we certainly swear that you are indeed insane!'" (وَ قالُوا يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِي نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ).

This expression portrays the ultimate insolence and audacity they displayed toward the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

On one hand, the use of the phrase "O you who!..." (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِي), on the other hand, the expression "to whom the Reminder has been sent down" (نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ) which they uttered in mockery and denial of the Quran, and thirdly, their emphasis on the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله insanity through the use of "Inna" (certainly) and the "Lam" of oath.

Indeed, when stubborn and shallow-minded individuals are confronted with a great and unparalleled intellect, one of the first labels they attach to him is insanity. This is because they use their own small and feeble intellect as the criterion, and whatever does not fit within this measure is perceived by them as lack of reason and madness!

Such individuals possess a particular prejudice toward the existing issues of their environment, even if it be misguidance and error. Therefore, they combat every new call as an irrational invitation; they are terrified of innovations and are strictly bound to false traditions.

Furthermore, worldlings who measure everything by material standards cannot believe that a person is sane if they encounter someone willing to lose all their material interests and even their life to achieve a spiritual goal. This is because "intellect" in their convention means accumulating more wealth, having a more beautiful spouse, a more comfortable life, and a higher apparent rank and position! It is perfectly clear that with such a mindset, if they see someone who, in the face of the best wealth, women, and positions, says: "If you place the sun in my right hand and the moon in my left, and if I were to rule over the entire solar system instead of your small environment, I would not abandon my call," they would have no choice but to call him insane.

And it is strange that these foolish people attached labels to the Divine leaders that were sometimes completely contradictory; sometimes they called them "insane" and sometimes "sorcerers," even though a sorcerer is someone who must possess a particular cleverness and alertness, which is the exact opposite of being insane.

They did not only attribute such foolish labels to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, but as a pretext, they would say: "If you are telling the truth, why do you not bring the angels to us!"

So that they may testify to your words and we may believe (لَوْ ما تَأْتِينا بِالْمَلائِكَةِ إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ).

Allah answers them as such: "We do not send down the angels except with the truth" (ما نُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ). "And if the angels were to descend (and the reality were to take on a witnessed and sensory aspect for them) and they still did not believe, they would no longer be granted any respite," and they would be destroyed by Divine punishment (وَ ما كانُوا إِذاً مُنْظَرِينَ).

In the commentary of the phrase "ما نُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ" (We do not send down the angels except with the truth), commentators have provided various explanations:

  1. Some have said it means that We will only perform the sending down of angels as a miracle for the manifestation of the truth, not as a pretext for those who would see and still not believe. In other words, a miracle is not a plaything to be subject to arbitrary demands; rather, it is for proving the truth, and this matter has been sufficiently proven for those who seek the truth, as the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله has established his mission by having this Quran and other miracles in hand.

  2. The "truth" refers to the final worldly punishment and the annihilating calamity—in other words, the "punishment of extirpation" (adhab al-istisal). That is, if the angels descend and they still do not believe—which they would not, given the stubbornness they possess—it would be coupled with their destruction.

The second sentence of the verse (وَ ما كانُوا إِذاً مُنْظَرِينَ) is also an emphasis on this same meaning. However, according to the first commentary, it conveys a new point.

  1. Another possibility is that "truth" means "death," meaning that angels only descend at the time of death and the taking of the soul, and not at any other time.

However, this commentary seems very unlikely, as we read in the Quran in the stories of Ibrahim and Lut عليهم السلام, and even regarding the Muslims in some battles, that angels descended upon them.

  1. "Truth" means "witnessing" (shuhud); that is, as long as one is in this world, veils cover his eyes, and he is unable to see such realities related to the metaphysical world. Only in the other world, where the veils are removed and it is the realm of witnessing, can he see the Divine angels.

This commentary also faces the same problem as the third commentary, because even the people of Lut, who were faithless and misguided individuals, saw the angels commissioned for punishment in this world [1]. Therefore, only the first and second commentaries are consistent with the apparent meaning of the verse.

As for why we read at the end of this verse that if, after all these clear proofs, their demand for a sensory miracle were granted and they still did not believe, they would no longer be granted respite, it is because in such a state, the proof (hujjah) is completed for them in every sense, and all excuses are cut off. Since the respite of life is for the purpose of completing the proof and the possibility of reconsidering and returning to the truth—and such a thing is meaningless for these types of individuals—the end of their lives is announced, and they reach the punishment they deserve (reflect carefully).

[1]: Surah Hud, Verse 81.

Ayah

إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا ٱلذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَـٰفِظُونَ (٩)
Indeed We have sent down the Reminder, and indeed We will preserve it. (9)

Commentary

Protection of the Quran from Tampering!

Following the excuses of the disbelievers and even their mockery of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Quran, as mentioned in the previous verses, the verse under discussion states a great and significant reality as a consolation to the Prophet on one hand, and as a reassurance to all true believers on the other:

"Indeed, it is We who sent down the Message [the Quran], and indeed, We will be its guardian" (إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَ إِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ‌).

It is not as if this Quran is without a supporter, or that they could cover the sun of its existence with mud, or extinguish its light and rays by blowing at it; for this is a lamp that God has lit, and this is a sun that shall have no setting or eclipse.

Let alone this small and weak group—even if all the tyrants, the powerful, the oppressive politicians, the deviant thinkers, and the warriors of the world joined hands to extinguish its light, they would not have the power, because Allah has taken upon Himself its protection and guardianship.

Regarding what exactly the protection and guardianship of the Quran refers to, commentators have offered various interpretations:

1- Some have said it refers to protection against distortion (tahrif), alteration, addition, and omission.

2- Others have said it refers to protection against extinction and destruction until the end of the world.

3- Others have said it refers to protection against misleading anti-Quranic logic.

However, these interpretations not only do not contradict each other but are all included within the general concept of "إِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ‌" (Indeed, We will be its guardian). Why should we confine and limit this protection and guardianship—which is stated in an absolute form and, as they say, with the omission of a specific object—to one particular dimension?

The truth is that, according to the apparent meaning of the above verse, Allah has promised to protect and guard it from every aspect: protection from any kind of distortion, protection from extinction and destruction, and protection from the sophistries of whispering enemies.

As for the possibility suggested by some earlier commentators that the intended meaning is the protection of the "Prophet" himself, and that the pronoun "lehu" (for him/it) refers to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—because the word "Dhikr" (Message/Reminder) is applied to the person of the Prophet in some verses of the Quran (Surah At-Talaq, Verse 10)—this seems very unlikely.

This is because in the verses preceding the one under discussion, the word "Dhikr" explicitly refers to the "Quran," and this verse following it undoubtedly refers to the same meaning.

Discussion on the Non-Distortion of the Quran

It is well-known and famous among all scholars of the Shi'a and the Ahl al-Sunnah that no distortion whatsoever has occurred in the Quran. The Quran that is in our hands today is exactly the same Quran that was revealed to the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and not even a word or a letter has been added to or taken from it.

Among the great Shi'a scholars of the past and present, the following can be named as those who have explicitly stated this truth:

1- The late Sheikh Tusi, known as Sheikh al-Ta'ifa; at the beginning of his famous commentary (Al-Tibyan), he has a clear, explicit, and decisive discussion on this matter.

2- Sayyid Murtada, who was among the greatest scholars of the Imamiyyah in the fourth century AH.

3- The Chief of Traditionists, the late Saduq, Muhammad ibn Ali ibn Babawayh; in stating the beliefs of the Imamiyyah, he says: "Our belief is that no distortion has occurred in the Quran."

4- The great commentator, the late Tabarsi, also has an eloquent discussion on this matter in the introduction to his commentary.

5- The late Kashif al-Ghita, who is among the great scholars of the later generations.

6- The late Muhaqqiq Yazdi, in the book Al-Urwat al-Wuthqa, narrates the non-distortion of the Quran from the majority of Shi'a jurists.

7- This belief has also been narrated from many other great figures such as "Sheikh Mufid," "Sheikh Baha'i," "Qadi Nurullah," and other Shi'a researchers.

The great scholars and researchers of the Ahl al-Sunnah are also mostly of this same opinion.

Although the occurrence of distortion in the Quran has been narrated from some traditionists (muhaddithin) of the Shi'a and the Ahl al-Sunnah who had incomplete knowledge regarding the Quran, this belief has been invalidated and consigned to oblivion through the enlightenment provided by the great scholars of both schools.

To the extent that the late Sayyid Murtada, in response to "Al-Masa'il al-Tarabulsiyat," says: "The authenticity of the transmission of the Quran is so clear and evident that it is like our knowledge of the famous cities of the world, great historical events, and well-known books."

Can anyone, for example, doubt the existence of cities like Mecca and Medina, or London and Paris, even if they have never traveled to any of these cities?! And can anyone deny the Mongol invasion of Iran, the French Revolution, or the First and Second World Wars?! They cannot, because all of these have reached us through mass-transmission (tawatur). The verses of the Quran are exactly the same, as we will explain later.

If malicious individuals have tried to attribute the belief in distortion to the Shi'a to create division between the Shi'a and the Ahl al-Sunnah, the great books of the Shi'a scholars are proof of the falsehood of their claim.

It is not strange that an individual like "Fakhr Razi"—who we know has a particular sensitivity and prejudice regarding "Shi'a" matters—says under the verse in question that this verse (إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَ إِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ‌) is proof of the invalidity of the Shi'a view, who claim there is alteration, addition, and omission in the Quran! It must be said explicitly: if he means the great and researching scholars of the Shi'a, none of them held or hold such a belief; and if he means the existence of a weak opinion in this regard among the Shi'a, a similar thing exists among the Ahl al-Sunnah, which neither they have paid attention to, nor have we.

The famous researcher "Kashif al-Ghita" says in his book Kashf al-Ghita:

"There is no doubt that it (the Quran) is protected from omission by the protection of the Sovereign Judge, as indicated by the explicit text of the Quran and the consensus of scholars in every age; no weight is given to rare [dissenting] views." (Tafsir Ala' al-Rahman, page 35).

Islamic history has seen many such unjust attributions that have no source other than prejudice. We know that the factor behind some of these misunderstandings was enemies who fanned the flames of such issues, trying to ensure that unity was never established among the ranks of Muslims.

It reached the point where the famous Hijazi writer "Abdullah Ali al-Qasimi," in his book Al-Sira', while disparaging the Shi'a, says:

"The Shi'a have always been enemies of mosques! For this reason, one who travels the length and breadth of their lands, from north to south and from east to west, will rarely see a mosque!" [^1] Think carefully: we grow tired of counting the sheer number of mosques in the streets, alleys, markets, and even back-alleys in Shi'a-populated cities. Sometimes there are so many mosques in one spot that some people raise their voices saying, "Enough, let us attend to other matters." Yet, we see a famous writer speaking with such explicitness about something that is merely a cause for laughter for us who live in these regions. Therefore, one should not be too surprised by the attribution made by Fakhr Razi either.

Proofs of the Non-Distortion of the Quran

1- We have numerous proofs regarding the non-distortion of the Quran, the clearest of which—after the above verse and some other verses of the Quran—is the historical trajectory of this great Heavenly Book.

As a preliminary, it is necessary to remind that the weak minority who suggested the possibility of distortion in the Quran only did so regarding its "omission"; otherwise, no one has ever suggested the possibility that anything has been added to the existing Quran (pay close attention).

Beyond this, if we consider that the Quran was everything to the early Muslims—their constitution, their manual for life, their program of government, their sacred Heavenly Book, and the secret of their worship—it becomes clear that, fundamentally, addition or omission in it was impossible.

The Quran was a book that the early Muslims constantly used in prayers, in the mosque, at home, on the battlefield, when facing enemies, and as an argument for the truth of their school of thought. It is even found in Islamic histories that they would set the teaching of the Quran as a woman's dowry! Fundamentally, the only book discussed in all gatherings, which every child was introduced to from the beginning of their life, and which was taught to anyone who wanted to learn a lesson from Islam, was this very Glorious Quran.

With such a situation, does anyone consider it possible for a change to occur in this Heavenly Book? Especially since we proved at the beginning of the first volume of this commentary that the Quran was collected as a single volume in its current form during the time of the Prophet himself, and Muslims were intensely committed to learning and memorizing it. Fundamentally, a person's status in that era was largely recognized by how many verses of the Quran they had memorized.

The number of memorizers (huffaz) of the Quran was so large that we read in history that in one of the battles during the time of Abu Bakr, four hundred reciters (qurra') of the Quran were killed [^1]. In the story of "Bi'r Ma'una" (a settlement near Medina) and the battle that took place in that region during the life of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that a large group of Quran reciters from the companions of the Prophet—about 70 people—drank the nectar of martyrdom [^1]. From these and similar accounts, it is clear that the memorizers, reciters, and teachers of the Quran were so numerous that this many of them were martyred in just one battlefield.

And it must be so, because as we said, the Quran was not just a constitution for Muslims; rather, it constituted their everything. Especially at the dawn of Islam, they had no book other than it, and recitation, reading, memorization, teaching, and learning were exclusive to the Quran.

The Quran was not a neglected book in a corner of a house or a mosque with the dust of forgetfulness settled upon it, such that someone could take from it or add to it.

The practice of memorizing the Quran as a tradition and a great act of worship has always been and remains among Muslims. Even after the Quran was mass-produced as a book and distributed everywhere, and even after the advent of the printing industry—which caused this book to be printed and published as the most widely circulated book in Islamic countries—the matter of memorizing the Quran as an ancient tradition and a great honor maintained its position, such that in every city and land, there has always been a group of Quran memorizers.

Even now, in the Hijaz and some other Islamic countries, there are schools called "Madrasat Tahfiz al-Quran al-Karim" or other names, where the primary program for students is the memorization of the Quran.

In a meeting that took place during a trip to Mecca with the heads of these schools in that holy city, it became clear that a very large group of young boys and girls in Mecca had enrolled in these schools and were studying.

One informed person said that currently in the country of Pakistan, there are about one million and five hundred thousand Quran memorizers! One of the conditions for the entrance exam of Al-Azhar Islamic University in Egypt (according to the Encyclopedia of Farid Wajdi) is memorizing the entire Quran, for which they must score at least twenty out of forty marks!

In short, the tradition of memorizing the Quran has continued through all centuries and ages since the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, by his own command and emphasis, which has been recorded in many narrations.

With such a situation, is any possibility regarding the distortion of the Quran feasible?!

2- In addition to all this is the matter of the "Scribes of Revelation"—those who would record the verses of the Quran after they were revealed to the Prophet. Their number has been recorded as being between fourteen and forty-three people.

Abu Abdullah Zanjani says in his valuable book History of the Quran: "The Prophet had scribes who wrote the revelation, and they were forty-three; the most famous of them were the first four Caliphs, and those most constantly with the Prophet were Zaid ibn Thabit and Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام."

How is it possible for the hand of distorters to reach a book that had so many scribes?!

3- The invitation of all leaders of Islam to the existing Quran—it is noteworthy that an examination of the words of the great leaders of Islam shows that from the very beginning of Islam, they all spoke with one voice, inviting people to recite, study, and act upon this very existing Quran. This itself shows that this Heavenly Book existed as an untouched collection in all centuries, including the first centuries of Islam.

The words of Ali عليه السلام in Nahj al-Balagha are living testimony to this claim:

In Sermon 133, we read: "And the Book of Allah is among you; a speaker whose tongue never tires, a house whose pillars are never demolished, and a source of honor whose allies are never defeated."

In Sermon 176, he says: "And know that this Quran is the advisor that never deceives, and the guide that never misleads."

And also in the same sermon, we read: "No one sits with this Quran except that they rise from it with an increase or a decrease: an increase in guidance, or a decrease in blindness!"

Further in the same sermon, we read: "Indeed, Allah the Glorified has not exhorted anyone with the like of this Quran, for it is the firm rope of Allah and His trustworthy means."

And in Sermon 198, we read: "Then He sent down to him the Book as a light whose lamps are never extinguished, a lantern whose blaze does not fade... a path whose course does not mislead... and a criterion whose proof is never dampened."

Similar expressions are abundant in the words of Ali عليه السلام and other leaders of the religion.

If we were to assume that the hand of distortion had reached this Heavenly Book, would it be possible to invite people toward it in this manner? And to introduce it as the way, the method, the means of separating truth from falsehood, a light that is never extinguished, a lamp that never fades, the firm rope of God, and His faithful and trustworthy means?

4- Fundamentally, after accepting the finality of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and that the religion of Islam is the final divine religion, and that the mission of the Quran will remain until the end of the world, how can one believe that God would not guard this unique document of Islam and the Final Prophet?! Can the distortion of the Quran have any meaning alongside the eternity of Islam over thousands of years and until the end of the world?!

5- The narrations of Thaqalayn (the Two Weighty Things), which have been narrated through multiple authentic chains from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, are another proof of the authenticity of the Quran and its protection from any alteration. According to these narrations, the Prophet says: "I am leaving you, and I leave behind two weighty things: first, the Quran, and the other is my Ahl al-Bayt; if you do not let go of these two, you will never go astray." [^1] Can this expression be correct regarding a book that the hand of distortion has reached? [^2]

6- In addition to all this, the Quran has been introduced to Muslims as a reliable criterion for measuring the truth and falsehood of reports. In many narrations found in Islamic sources, we read: "If you doubt the truth or falsehood of any hadith, present it to the Quran; any hadith that agrees with the Quran is true, and any hadith that contradicts it is false!" If, hypothetically, distortion had occurred in the Quran—even in the form of omission—it would never have been possible to introduce it as the criterion for measuring truth from falsehood and correct hadith from incorrect ones.

Narrations of Distortion

The most important pretext that some have found for the issue of distortion is various narrations which, due to a lack of understanding of their real meaning or a lack of investigation into their chains of transmission, have led to such misinterpretations.

These narrations are of several types:

1- A portion of these narrations are those that say Ali عليه السلام began collecting the Quran after the passing of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and when he had collected it and presented it to a group of companions who surrounded the seat of the Caliphate, they did not welcome it, and Ali عليه السلام said they would not see it again!

However, with careful attention to these very narrations, it becomes clear that the Quran with Ali عليه السلام was not different from other Qurans at all; rather, the difference was in three things: first, that the verses and surahs were organized in the order of revelation; second, that the occasion of revelation (shan al-nuzul) of each verse and surah was noted alongside it; and third, that the interpretations he had heard from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, as well as the abrogating and abrogated verses, were included in it.

Therefore, the Quran that Ali عليه السلام set out to collect was nothing other than this Quran, and the "extra" parts were "interpretations," "explanations," "occasions of revelation," "distinction between abrogating and abrogated," and the like. In other words, it was both the Quran and the original commentary of the Quran.

In the Book of Sulaym ibn Qays, we read: "When the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام saw the betrayal of the companions and their lack of loyalty, he stayed in his house and turned to the Quran. When he had collected it all, writing it with his own hand along with its interpretation and the abrogating and abrogated, they sent for him to come out and pledge allegiance. He sent back saying: 'I am busy; I have sworn to myself not to put on my cloak except for prayer until I have compiled and collected the Quran.'" [^1]

2- Another portion of these narrations are those that refer to the "spiritual distortion" (tahrif ma'nawi) of the Quran.

For we know that distortion is of three types: "verbal distortion," "spiritual distortion," and "practical distortion."

Verbal distortion means that words and phrases of the Quran are added to, taken from, or changed (this is what we and all Islamic researchers strongly deny).

However, "spiritual distortion" means that a verse is interpreted and explained in a way that contradicts its real meaning. And "practical distortion" means acting contrary to it.

For example, in the commentary of Ali ibn Ibrahim, we read from "Abu Dharr": When the verse "The Day when some faces will turn white and some faces will turn black" (Al-Imran 106) was revealed, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "On the Day of Resurrection, people will be asked: what did you do with the Thaqalayn (the Quran and the Prophet's progeny)?" They will say: "As for the greater one (the Quran), we distorted it and threw it behind our backs..." [^1] It is clear that the meaning of distortion here is changing the concept of the Quran and casting it aside.

3- Another portion of these narrations are those that are certainly fabricated and were crafted by enemies, deviants, or ignorant individuals to discredit the Quran.

Such as the numerous narrations narrated from Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Sayyari, the number of which reaches one hundred and eighty-eight narrations! [^2] The late Hajji Nuri quoted extensively from him in the book Fasl al-Khitab.

"Sayyari," the narrator of these hadiths, was—according to many great scholars of biographical evaluation (rijal)—someone of corrupt belief, unreliable, and weak in hadith. According to some, he was an extremist (ghali), deviant, known for believing in reincarnation, and a liar. According to Kashi, the famous author of Rijal, Imam Jawad عليه السلام in his letter called Sayyari's claims false and baseless.

Of course, the narrations of distortion are not limited to Sayyari, but a significant portion of them traces back to him!

Among these fabricated narrations, ridiculous reports are seen that anyone with a modicum of study would immediately recognize as corrupt.

Like the narration that says in Verse 3 of Surah An-Nisa ("And if you fear that you will not deal justly with the orphan girls, then marry those who please you of [other] women"), more than one-third of the Quran has been dropped between the condition and the consequence!!! Whereas we said in the commentary of Surah An-Nisa that the condition and consequence in the above verse are perfectly related to each other, and not even a single word has been dropped between the two.

Furthermore, more than one-third of the Quran is equivalent to at least fourteen juz (parts) of the Quran! It is very laughable for someone to claim that the Quran—with all those scribes and writers of revelation and all those memorizers and reciters from the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله onward—had fourteen of its parts vanish without anyone noticing. These liars and fabricators did not pay attention to the historical reality that the Quran, which formed the constitution and everything for Muslims from the beginning, was recited day and night in all houses and mosques; it was not something from which even one word could be dropped, let alone fourteen parts! A lie of this magnitude is clear evidence of the amateurishness of the fabricators of such hadiths.

Many excuse-seekers here turn to the book Fasl al-Khitab mentioned above (written by the late Hajji Nuri), which was written regarding distortion.

However, regarding this book—in addition to its status being clear from what we said above—the late Haj Sheikh Agha Bozorg Tehrani, who was a prominent student of the late Hajji Nuri, says in the biography of his teacher in the first volume of Mustadrak al-Wasa'il:

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ فِى شِيَعِ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٠)
Certainly We sent [apostles] before you to former communities, (10)
وَمَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (١١)
and there did not come to them any apostle but that they used to deride him. (11)
كَذَٰلِكَ نَسْلُكُهُۥ فِى قُلُوبِ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (١٢)
That is how We let it pass through the hearts of the guilty: (12)
لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ ۖ وَقَدْ خَلَتْ سُنَّةُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٣)
they do not believe in it, and the precedent of the ancients has already passed. (13)
وَلَوْ فَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِم بَابًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَظَلُّوا۟ فِيهِ يَعْرُجُونَ (١٤)
Were We to open for them a gate of the sky, so that they could go on ascending through it, (14)

they would surely say, ‘Indeed a spell has been cast on our eyes; indeed, we are a bewitched lot.’ (15)

Commentary

Obstinacy and the Denial of the Perceptible

In these verses, as a consolation to the Prophet and the believers regarding the difficulties they faced in their mission, reference is made to the lives of previous prophets and their struggles against misguided and prejudiced nations.

First, It says: "And We certainly sent [messengers] before you among the sects of the former peoples" (وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ فِي شِيَعِ الْأَوَّلِينَ).

But they were so obstinate and stubborn that "no messenger would come to them except that they ridiculed him" (وَمَا يَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا كَانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِئُونَ). This ridicule and mockery were due to several factors:

  • To break the grandeur of the prophets and to disperse truth-seekers from around them.

  • Due to their own weakness and inability in the face of the powerful logic of the Divine messengers; since they could not provide an answer to their crushing arguments, they resorted to ridicule—the weapon of the logicless ignorant.

  • Because the prophets were breakers of [false] traditions and rose to challenge the disordered state of the environment. The prejudiced ignorant, who considered these wrong traditions to be eternal and everlasting, were astonished by this and rose in mockery.

  • To place a cover over their sleeping conscience, lest it wake up and create commitment and responsibility.

  • Because many of the prophets were empty-handed regarding worldly wealth and lived very simple lives. Those who, due to spiritual blindness, perceived personality in new clothes, beautiful mounts, and a luxurious life, wondered how a poor and destitute man could become the leader and guide of such a wealthy and affluent population?! Consequently, they would resort to ridicule.

  • And finally, because they saw that accepting the prophets' call would limit them in their desires, strip away their animalistic freedoms, and create duties and responsibilities for them, they resorted to mockery to rid themselves of these obligations!

Then He says: Yes, "Thus We let it [the Quran] pass into the hearts of the criminals" (كَذَلِكَ نَسْلُكُهُ فِي قُلُوبِ الْمُجْرِمِينَ).

Yet, despite all this preaching, emphasis, logical explanation, and the presentation of miracles, these mocking bigots still "do not believe in it" (لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ). But this is not unique to them; "and the precedent of the former peoples has already passed" (وَقَدْ خَلَتْ سُنَّةُ الْأَوَّلِينَ).

Due to being immersed in desires and their persistence and obstinacy in falsehood, they have reached a point where "even if We opened for them a gate to the heaven and they were to continue ascending through it..." (وَلَوْ فَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ بَابًا مِنَ السَّمَاءِ فَظَلُّوا فِيهِ يَعْرُجُونَ).

"They would say, 'Our eyes have only been dazzled'" (لَقَالُوا إِنَّمَا سُكِّرَتْ أَبْصَارُنَا). "Rather, we are a people affected by magic," and what we see has no reality at all! (بَلْ نَحْنُ قَوْمٌ مَسْحُورُونَ).

It is not surprising that a human reaches such a stage of defiance and obstinacy. This is because the pure spirit and untouched nature of man, which is capable of perceiving truths and observing the true face of realities, gradually turns toward darkness due to sin, ignorance, and enmity toward the truth. Of course, in the early stages, purifying it is entirely possible; however, if—God forbid—this state becomes deep-rooted in a person and turns into a "habitual trait" (malakah), it can no longer be easily washed away. It is here that the face of truth becomes distorted in the person's view, to the point that the strongest intellectual proofs and the clearest sensory evidences have no effect on his heart, and his condition leads to the denial of both the rational and the perceptible.

Key Points

1- The Lexical Meaning of "Shiya'"

"Shiya'" (شِيَع) is the plural of "Shi'ah" (شيعة), referring to a population or group that shares a common line or path. Al-Raghib says in the book Al-Mufradat: "Shi'ah is derived from the root shiya' (شياع), meaning 'spread and strengthening.' Sha'a al-khabar is said when a report becomes frequent and strong, and sha'a al-qawm is said when a population becomes widespread and numerous. 'Shi'ah' refers to those through whom a person becomes strong."

The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan considers its origin to be from mushaya'ah (مشايعت), meaning "following," and says Shi'ah means a follower and adherent. The Shi'ah of Ali (عليه السلام) refers to his followers and those who believe in his Imamate. The famous Hadith of Umm Salama from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "شيعة علي هم الفائزون يوم القيامة" (The followers of Ali (عليه السلام) are the successful ones on the Day of Resurrection) also points to this same meaning.

In any case, whether we consider the root of this word to be from shiya' meaning spreading and strengthening, or from mushaya'ah meaning following, it indicates the existence of a type of intellectual and ideological solidarity in the concept of Shi'ah and Tashayyu'.

Furthermore, the use of the term shiya' regarding past nations is evidence that they did not act in a scattered manner in their struggle against the prophets; rather, they had a common line and a single program that was strengthened by coordinated actions. If the misguided have such a program for themselves, should not the true followers of the path of truth follow coordinated and shared plans in their journey?

2- The Referent of the Pronoun in "Naslukuhu"

This phrase indicates that Allah conveys His verses to the criminals and opponents through various means so clearly that it is as if they have entered their hearts. However, unfortunately, the lack of capacity and unreadiness of the "vessel" causes it to be expelled and not absorbed into the depths of their souls—much like nutritious and beneficial food that an unhealthy stomach does not absorb and returns (this reality is precisely understood from the root suluk, which is the base of naslukuhu).

Therefore, the pronoun in naslukuhu refers to the "Dhikr" (the Quran) mentioned in the previous verses. Similarly, the pronoun in the following phrase, "they do not believe in it" (la yu'minuna bihi), also refers to the same meaning—that is, despite all this, they do not believe in these verses.

Thus, harmony between the two pronouns is perfectly maintained. A similar expression with the same meaning is seen in Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verses 200 and 201.

However, some have suggested the possibility that the pronoun in naslukuhu refers to "ridicule," which is inferred from the previous verse. Thus, the meaning of the sentence would be: "We cause this ridicule and mockery to enter their hearts (due to their sins and obstinacy)."

But this interpretation, if it has no other flaws, at least destroys the harmony between the two pronouns, and this is sufficient to weaken it (reflect on this).

Additionally, it is understood from the above sentence that the duty of preachers is not merely to recite matters into people's ears; rather, they must use all means to make the word of truth penetrate their hearts so that it becomes heart-settling—so that truth-seekers are guided and the argument is completed against the obstinate.

That is, one must utilize all audio-visual means, practical programs, stories, narratives, literature, poetry, and art in its authentic and constructive sense so that the words of truth find their way into hearts.

3- The Precedent of the Ancients!

The stances of the supporters of falsehood against the prophets, and the designing of comprehensive plans to fool the people and disperse them from around the men of God, is neither a new thing nor limited to a specific time or place.

Rather, as understood from the expression above, these conspiracies have existed among misguided nations since ancient times and the most distant eras.

For this reason, one should never be terrified by it, nor allow hopelessness and despair to creep in, nor fear the multitude of problems created by the enemies. This is an effective consolation for all wayfarers of the path of truth.

If we imagine that in some time or place we can publish and expand the call of truth and raise the banner of justice without facing the negative reaction of obstinate enemies and fierce opponents, we are gravely mistaken. The Divine prophets and all their true followers never despaired of these oppositions, and we too must not despair; rather, we must increase the depth of our call every day.

4- Commentary on the Phrase "فَظَلُّوا فِيهِ يَعْرُجُونَ"

This phrase and the subsequent phrases in the above verses clearly show that the meaning is: even if a gate from the heaven were opened for them (the "heaven" here apparently refers to the dense atmosphere around the earth, which is not easily exited) and they were to repeatedly come and go through it during the bright day, they would still say, out of the intensity of their obstinacy, that they have been dazzled or bewitched.

Note that zhallu (ظلوا) is evidence of the continuation of an action during the day, and the Arabs do not use this expression for the night; instead, they use batu (باتوا), which is from the root baytutah.

This interpretation is what most commentators have chosen. However, it is strange that some commentators have suggested that the pronoun in zhallu refers to the angels—meaning if they were to see the angels with their own eyes ascending to heaven and returning, they still would not believe.

This interpretation, besides being inconsistent with the preceding and succeeding verses which generally speak of the polytheists (as angels were mentioned only six verses prior, and returning the pronoun to them is very remote), also diminishes the eloquence of the speech. For the Quran intends to say that even if they themselves, in a miraculous manner, were to repeatedly go to heaven and return in broad daylight, they would still not submit to the truth (reflect on this).

5- Meaning of the Phrase "Sukkirat Absaruna"

Sukkirat (سُكِّرَتْ) is from the root sukr, meaning "to cover." That is, the obstinate disbelievers say: "Our truth-perceiving eyes have seemingly been covered, and if we see ourselves ascending to heaven, it is a series of imaginary and illusory matters." This is exactly what we refer to in Persian as "chashm-bandi" (sleight of hand/dazzling), where due to the opponent's dexterity, a person cannot see the truth correctly but rather perceives the opposite.

The fact that the phrase "Rather, we are a people affected by magic" (bal nahnu qawmun mashurun) follows it—even though "dazzling" is also a type of magic—perhaps indicates that it has gone beyond mere dazzling; our entire being has been bewitched. Not only our eyes, but our entire existence has lost its true perception due to magic, and what it senses is contrary to reality.

In other words, whenever a person is lifted and lowered by some means, he senses this state not only with his eyes but with his entire being. For this reason, even if a person's eyes are tightly shut, he still senses his ascent and descent.

That is, if, hypothetically, we were to take these polytheists to heaven, they would first say "we have been dazzled," and then, when they realize that this state is perceivable by means other than the eyes, they would say that fundamentally, our entire existence from head to toe has been bewitched.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ جَعَلْنَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ بُرُوجًا وَزَيَّنَّـٰهَا لِلنَّـٰظِرِينَ (١٦)
Certainly We have appointed houses in the sky and adorned them for the onlookers, (16)
وَحَفِظْنَـٰهَا مِن كُلِّ شَيْطَـٰنٍ رَّجِيمٍ (١٧)
and We have guarded them from every outcast Satan, (17)
إِلَّا مَنِ ٱسْتَرَقَ ٱلسَّمْعَ فَأَتْبَعَهُۥ شِهَابٌ مُّبِينٌ (١٨)
except someone who may eavesdrop, whereat there pursues him a manifest flame. (18)

Commentary

Are Satans Driven Away by Meteors?

These verses point to a corner of the system of creation as a proof of monotheism and the recognition of God, completing the discussions regarding the Quran and prophethood that appeared in the preceding verses.

First, It says: "And We have placed constellations in the heaven" (وَلَقَدْ جَعَلْنَا فِي السَّمَاءِ بُرُوجًا).

"Buruj" (بروج) is the plural of "Burj" (برج), which originally means "manifestation" or "visibility." For this reason, that specific part of a city wall or a military gathering place that has a particular prominence and visibility is called a Burj (tower). Similarly, when a woman displays her adornments, it is said: tabarrajat al-mar'ah.

In any case, the celestial constellations refer to the stations of the sun and the moon. More precisely, when we look at the moon and the sun from the planet Earth, in every season and time of the year, we see them positioned against one of the constellations (constellations are groups of stars that have taken on a specific shape). We say, for example, that the sun is in the constellation of Aries, Taurus, Libra, Scorpio, or Sagittarius [1]. Clearly, the existence of these celestial constellations, the stations of the moon and sun, and the specific system governing their movement within these constellations—which forms the living calendar of our universe—is a clear proof of the knowledge and power of the Creator. This magnificent system, with such precise and continuous calculation, reflects a plan and a purpose behind the creation of this world; the more we reflect upon it, the closer it brings us to the Creator of the universe.

Then It adds: "And We have beautified it for the observers" (وَزَيَّنَّاهَا لِلنَّاظِرِينَ) [2]. On a dark, starry night, cast a glance at the sky; stars gathered in groups in every corner, as if each group has its own assembly, whispering softly. Some stare at us fixedly without twinkling at all, while others twinkle constantly, as if calling us toward them. Some shine so brightly that we imagine they are approaching us, while others, with their faint light, seem to let out a silent cry from the depths of the distant heavens: "We are here too!" This beautiful, poetic scene—which may seem ordinary to some due to repeated viewing—is more worth seeing, interesting, and soul-stirring than we might think.

[^1]: Aries (the Ram) is a group of stars that has roughly taken this shape in the sky; similarly, Taurus means the Bull, Libra means the Scales, Scorpio has its well-known meaning, and Sagittarius means the Bow.

[^2]: The pronoun in "zayyannaha" refers to "sama'" (heaven) because sama' is a feminine noun in Arabic grammar.

Sometimes, when the moon appears among them in its various phases, they take on another fresh and astonishing countenance. After sunset, they appear one after another, as if running out from behind a curtain, and at dawn, they flee and hide themselves before the dazzling power of the sun.

Beyond this, from the perspective of scientific beauty and countless mysteries, the face of the sky is so beautiful that it has occupied the eyes of all scientists for thousands of years—especially today, when they gaze toward it with the most powerful telescopes and massive astronomical cameras, discovering new secrets of this bustling yet silent world for the people of the earth every moment.

Truly, "The wheel of heaven with these stars is fine, pleasant, and beautiful!"...

In the next verse, It adds: "And We have guarded it from every accursed, ominous, and rejected Satan" (وَحَفِظْنَاهَا مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْطَانٍ رَجِيمٍ).

"Except him who steals a hearing (and eavesdrops), then a manifest flame follows him and drives him back" (إِلَّا مَنِ اسْتَرَقَ السَّمْعَ فَأَتْبَعَهُ شِهابٌ مُبِينٌ).

How are Satans driven from the sky by meteors? This last verse is among those about which commentators have spoken extensively, each following a specific path and having a particular interpretation. Since this same theme appears in Surah As-Saffat (verses 6 and 7) and Surah Al-Jinn (verse 9), and it is one of those issues that might raise unanswered questions for uninformed individuals, it is necessary first to look at the views of great Islamic commentators and then state what we prefer.

  1. Some, like the author of Tafsir Fi Zilal, have passed over these and similar verses easily, stating that these are truths whose comprehension is impossible for us, and that we should focus on what affects our actual practice in this life. He contented himself with a brief commentary and refrained from explaining or clarifying this issue.

He writes: "What is Satan? How does he want to eavesdrop? And what does he eavesdrop upon? All of these are from the divine mysteries (ghayb) that cannot be reached through the texts, and engaging with them yields no result, for it adds nothing to our creed. It would only result in occupying the human mind with a matter that has no specific relevance to him and hinders him from performing his true work in this life. Moreover, it gives us no new understanding of a new reality!" [1]

However, there should be no doubt that the Quran is a great book of human-building, education, and life. If something had no connection to human life, it would absolutely not be mentioned in it. All of it is a lesson—a lesson for life. Furthermore, no one can accept the claim that there are unopenable truths in the Quran. Is the Quran not a Light and a Manifest Book? Was it not revealed for the understanding, reflection, and guidance of the people? How can the understanding of these verses not concern us? In any case, we do not favor this stance toward such verses.

  1. A significant group of commentators, especially the earlier ones, insist on fully preserving the literal meaning of the verses:

"Sama'" (heaven) refers to this physical sky, and "Shihab" (meteor) refers to these very meteors (those wandering pebbles in this vast space that occasionally enter the Earth's gravitational field, are pulled toward Earth, and due to the speed of impact with air waves, become hot, burning, flaming, and turn to ash). And "Satan" refers to those rebellious, evil beings who want to go to the heavens and obtain a portion of the news of our world reflected in the heavens through eavesdropping (secret listening) and deliver it to their friends on Earth, but the meteors are hurled at them like arrows and prevent them from reaching this goal [1].

  1. Another group of commentators, such as the noble commentator, the author of Al-Mizan, and Tantawi in Al-Jawahir, consider the expressions in the above verses to be of the nature of metaphors, allusions, parables, and so-called "symbolic" language. They have explained this metaphor and allusion in various ways:

A: In Tafsir Al-Mizan, we read: "The various justifications that commentators have given for the eavesdropping of Satans and their being driven away by meteors are based on what occasionally comes to mind from the literal appearance of verses and narrations—that there are celestial spheres surrounding the Earth inhabited by groups of angels, and each of these spheres has doors that cannot be entered except through them, and a group of these angels hold meteors in their hands, waiting for the eavesdropping Satans to strike and drive them away! While today it has become clear that such theories are baseless; neither do such spheres exist, nor those doors, nor anything like them.

What can be said here as a possibility is that these types of statements in the Divine Word are among the parables mentioned to clarify non-sensory truths in sensory clothing, just as Allah says: 'And these parables We strike for mankind, and none understand them except the learned' (Al-Ankabut: 43). Such expressions are frequent in the Quran, such as the Throne (Arsh), the Chair (Kursi), the Tablet (Lawh), and the Book.

Therefore, the 'heaven' which is the abode of angels is a celestial and metaphysical world that is superior to and higher than this sensory world. The meaning of Satans approaching this heaven for eavesdropping and having meteors hurled at them is that they want to approach the world of angels to become aware of the secrets of creation and future events, but they are driven away by celestial, spiritual lights that they lack the capacity to endure" [1].

B: Tantawi, in his famous commentary, sometimes says: "That group of deceitful, hypocritical scholars and the common people who follow them do not possess the merit or eligibility to become aware of the wonders of the heavens, the marvels of the upper world, its boundless spheres, and the system and calculation that govern them. Allah has barred them from this knowledge and has placed this beautiful, adorned, starry sky with all its secrets at the disposal of those who possess intellect, intelligence, sincerity, and faith.

Naturally, the first group is prevented from penetrating the secrets of this heaven, and every Satan rejected from the Divine Presence—whether of human or non-human kind—has no right to reach these truths, and whenever he approaches them, he is driven away and rejected. Such individuals may live for years and then die, yet never reach these secrets. They have open eyes, but their eyes lack the power to see these truths. Is it not true that none comprehend science except its lovers, and none see beauty except its gnostics?" [1]

Elsewhere he says: "What prevents these expressions from being metaphors? Sensory prevention refers to intellectual prevention, while metaphors are among the most beautiful types of eloquence. Do we not see many people living around you who are confined and imprisoned within the limits of this Earth, their eyes never fixed on the upper world, never listening to its voice, and having no news of the affairs of this world and its wonders? They are rejected from perceiving those lofty meanings by the (meteor) of selfishness, lust, malice, greed, avarice, and devastating wars" (and if one day they have such a desire, they are driven away because of all the pollution they have in their hearts and souls) [2].

C: Elsewhere he has a statement whose summary is: When human souls transition from this world to the world of Barzakh, they have a connection and relationship with the souls of the living. Whenever there is a compatibility and similarity between them, and they can establish communication and understanding through the summoning of spirits or otherwise, they provide them with matters that are sometimes true and sometimes false, because they cannot access the higher worlds but only the lower worlds. For example, just as a fish cannot come out of its environment and fly in the air, they also lack the power to go higher than the limits of their own world.

D: Others have said that recent discoveries have shown that a series of powerful radio waves are constantly broadcast from distant spaces, which can be well-received on Earth with special receiving devices. No one knows exactly where the source of these exceptionally powerful waves is, but scientists say there is a strong possibility that there are many living beings in distant celestial spheres who are much more advanced than us in terms of civilization, and for this reason, they want to broadcast their news in the universe. In that news are matters that are new to us. Beings we call Jinn try to use these waves, but powerful rays drive them away [1].

These were the various views of commentators and scholars.

Key Points

The discussions regarding the interpretation of these verses have been lengthy. Now, to reach a complete conclusion, we must first pay attention to several points:

  1. The word "Sama'" (heaven/sky) in many verses of the Quran refers to this physical sky, while in some other verses, it certainly refers to the spiritual heaven and a high station. For example, in verse 40 of Surah Al-A'raf, we read: "Indeed, those who deny Our verses and are arrogant toward them—the gates of heaven will not be opened for them." It is possible that "heaven" here is a metaphor for the station of proximity to God, just as we read in verse 10 of Surah Fatir: "To Him ascends good speech, and righteous work raises it." It is clear that righteous deeds and pure words are not things that ascend toward this physical sky; rather, they progress toward the station of proximity to God and find spiritual greatness and loftiness.

Fundamentally, the use of "anzala" (sent down) and "nazzala" regarding the verses of the Quran clearly conveys this meaning—that the intent is "descent" from the station of the Lord's sanctity upon the heart of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family). In the commentary of verse 24 of Surah Ibrahim: "Have you not considered how Allah presents an example, [making] a good word like a good tree, whose root is firmly fixed and its branch is in the heaven," we read that this pure tree, which Allah has presented as an example—the Prophet is its root, and Ali (peace be upon him) is its branch (the very branch that has reached into the heaven), and the Imams are the smaller branches [1]. Specifically, in one of those hadiths, we read: "Likewise the disbelievers, their deeds do not ascend to the heaven." It is clear that "heaven" in these types of hadiths does not refer to this sensory sky. From this, we conclude that "heaven" is used both in its physical concept and its spiritual concept.

  1. "Nujum" (stars) also has a physical concept, which are these stars seen in the sky, and a spiritual concept, which refers to the scholars and personalities who provide light to human societies. Just as people find their way in dark nights in deserts and on the surface of oceans by means of the stars, the masses of people in human societies also find the path of life, happiness, and vitality with the help of these scholars and aware, faithful leaders. The famous hadith narrated from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family): "The example of my companions among you is like the stars; whichever of them you follow, you will be guided..." [1] also points to this same meaning.

In the commentary of Ali ibn Ibrahim regarding the verse: "And He it is who has placed for you the stars that you may be guided by them through the darknesses of the land and the sea" (Al-An'am: 97), it is narrated that the Imam said: "The stars are the Family of Muhammad" [2].

  1. From numerous narrations regarding the interpretation of the verses under discussion, it is understood that the barring of Satans from ascending to the heavens and their being driven away by stars occurred at the birth of the Prophet. From some of them, it is understood that they were partially barred at the birth of Jesus and were completely barred after the birth of the Prophet [3].

From the sum of what we have said, it can be surmised that "Sama'" here refers to the spiritual concept and is a metaphor for the heaven of Truth, Faith, and Spirituality. Satans are constantly striving to find a way into this realm and to penetrate the hearts of true believers and supporters of Truth through various temptations. However, the men of God and the leaders of the path of Truth—from the Prophets and Imams to the committed scholars—attack them with the powerful waves of their knowledge and piety and drive them away from approaching this heaven.

It is here that one can find a connection and link between the birth of Christ, and even more so the birth of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family), and the expulsion of these Satans from the heaven. It is also here that one can find the connection between ascending to heaven and awareness of secrets—for we know that in this physical sky, there is no specific news except the wonders of creation, which can be studied even from the Earth. Today, it has become certain that these celestial spheres are spread out in this vast space; some of them are dead spheres, and some are living and have inhabitants, though perhaps their life differs greatly from ours.

It is also very noteworthy that the occurrence of a "Shihab" (meteor) happens only within the Earth's atmosphere, caused by the impact of pieces of rock with the air surrounding the Earth and their ignition; otherwise, there are no meteors outside the Earth's atmosphere. Of course, wandering rocks move outside the atmosphere, but they are not called "meteors" until they enter the Earth's atmosphere, become hot and flaming, and appear before the human eye as a line of fire, such that it is imagined to be a moving star. We also know that modern man has repeatedly passed through this Earth's atmosphere to the outside, gone far beyond it, and reached the Moon (note that the Earth's atmosphere is no more than one hundred to two hundred kilometers, while the Moon is more than three hundred thousand kilometers away from us). Therefore, if the intent were this physical meteor and physical sky, it must be accepted that this region has been discovered by human scientists and contains no mysterious matter.

In short, with the numerous clues and evidences we have mentioned, it seems that the "heaven" refers to the heaven of Truth and Reality, and the "Satans" are the tempters who try to find a way into this heaven, eavesdrop, and engage in the seduction of people. But the "stars" and "meteors"—meaning the divine leaders and scholars—drive them back and reject them with the powerful waves of their pens. However, the Quran is a boundless ocean, and it is possible that future scientists will reach new truths regarding these verses that we have not reached today.

Ayahs

وَٱلْأَرْضَ مَدَدْنَـٰهَا وَأَلْقَيْنَا فِيهَا رَوَٰسِىَ وَأَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ مَّوْزُونٍ (١٩)
And We spread out the earth, and cast in it firm mountains, and We grew in it every kind of balanced thing, (19)
وَجَعَلْنَا لَكُمْ فِيهَا مَعَـٰيِشَ وَمَن لَّسْتُمْ لَهُۥ بِرَٰزِقِينَ (٢٠)
and made in it [various] means of livelihood for you and for those whom you do not provide for. (20)
وَإِن مِّن شَىْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَآئِنُهُۥ وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُۥٓ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ (٢١)
There is not a thing but that its sources are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure. (21)

Commentary

The Treasure of All Things is With Us

Here, the text addresses a portion of the verses of creation and the signs of Allah’s greatness on earth to complete the preceding discussion.

First, starting with the earth itself, He says: "And the earth We have spread out" (وَ الْأَرْضَ مَدَدْناها).

"Madd" (مد) originally means to expand and stretch out. In this context, it most likely refers to the emergence of the dry lands of the earth from beneath the water. For we know that in the beginning, the entire surface of the globe was hidden under water due to torrential rains. This continued for long ages until the torrential rains subsided, the waters settled into the hollows of the earth, and the dry lands gradually appeared from beneath the water. This is the same concept known in Islamic narrations as "Dahw al-Ard" (the spreading of the earth).

Since the creation of mountains, with their numerous benefits, is one of the signs of Monotheism (Tawhid), He mentions them and adds: "And We set therein firm and immovable mountains" (وَ أَلْقَيْنا فِيها رَواسِيَ).

The use of the term "Alqa" (casting/throwing) regarding mountains—even though we know mountains are folds in the earth's crust created by the gradual cooling of the crust or volcanic activity—might be because "Alqa" can also mean "to create." In our daily language, we say we "laid out" a plan for a piece of land and "threw in" (built) a few rooms, meaning we constructed and created them.

In any case, these mountains, besides being interconnected at their roots and acting like armor to protect the earth from internal pressure and tremors, and besides breaking the force of storms and precisely controlling the flow of winds and breezes, serve as excellent reservoirs for water in the form of snow or springs.

Specifically, the emphasis on the word "Rawasi" (plural of rasiyah), meaning firm and stable, is a subtle reference to what we mentioned above: that they are stable themselves and are the source of stability for the earth's crust and human life.

Next, He turns to the most important factor for the life of humans and all living beings—namely, plants—and says: "And We caused to grow therein every balanced thing" (وَ أَنْبَتْنا فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ مَوْزُونٍ).

What a beautiful and eloquent expression is "Mawzun" (balanced), which is originally derived from the root "Wazn" (weight/measure), meaning to identify the measure of something. [^1] It refers to the precise calculation, wonderful order, and proportionate measures in all parts of plants. Each plant, and indeed each of its parts—from the stem, branch, leaf, and petal to the seed and fruit—follows a specific calculation and design.

On planet Earth, there are perhaps hundreds of thousands of types of plants with different properties and diverse effects, the study of each being a window to knowing "Allah," and the leaf of each being a ledger of "knowledge of the Creator."

There is also a possibility that this sentence refers to the "growth" of various minerals within mountains, as the Arabs also use the word "Inbat" (growing) regarding minerals.

Some narrations also point to this meaning. In a narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read that when he was asked about the interpretation of this verse, he said: "It means that Allah created in the mountains mines of gold, silver, jewels, and other metals." [^2] It is also possible that "Inbat" (growing) here has a broad meaning encompassing all creatures Allah has brought into existence on earth. In Surah Nuh, we read the words of this great Prophet to the people: وَ اللَّهُ أَنْبَتَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ نَباتاً: "And Allah has caused you to grow from the earth as a plant" (Nuh: 17).

[^1]: Al-Wazn is knowing the measure of a thing (Mufradat al-Raghib).

[^2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 6 (Note that according to this interpretation, the pronoun "therein" refers back to the mountains).

In any case, there is no obstacle to the verse having a broad concept that includes plants, humans, minerals, and the like.

Since the means of human life are not limited to plants and minerals, the next verse refers to all these blessings, saying: "And We have provided therein means of subsistence for you" (وَ جَعَلْنا لَكُمْ فِيها مَعايِشَ). Not only for you but for all living creatures: "and for those for whom you are not the providers" and who are beyond your reach (وَ مَنْ لَسْتُمْ لَهُ بِرازِقِينَ).

Yes, for all of them, We have provided their needs.

"Ma'ayish" (معايش) is the plural of "Ma'ishah" (معيشة), referring to the means and necessities of human life, which sometimes he seeks out himself, and sometimes they come to him.

Although some commentators have interpreted "Ma'ayish" only as agriculture and plants or food and drink, it is clear that the linguistic concept is quite broad and includes all means of life.

Commentators have offered two interpretations for the phrase "those for whom you are not the providers" (مَنْ لَسْتُمْ لَهُ بِرازِقِينَ):

First, as mentioned above, Allah wishes to describe His blessings toward both humans and the animals and living creatures that humans are unable to feed.

Second, Allah wishes to remind humans that We have placed both the means of life for you on this earth and have placed living creatures at your disposal (like livestock) for which you do not have the power to provide sustenance; rather, Allah provides for them, even if this task is carried out by your hands. [^1] However, in our view, the first interpretation seems more correct, and we have provided the linguistic reason for it in the footnote.

In a tradition narrated from the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, we find support for this interpretation where, regarding the meaning of the phrase "those for whom you are not the providers," it says: "For every kind of animal, We have ordained a measured provision." [^2]

In the final verse under discussion, He essentially answers a question that arises for many people: why does Allah not place so much provision and blessings at the disposal of humans that they would be independent of any effort or struggle?
