He says: "And there is not a thing but with Us are its treasuries, and We do not send it down except in a known measure" (وَ إِنْ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَ ما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ).

[^1]: According to the first interpretation, "man" (those) in the phrase "those for whom you are not the providers" is conjoined to the pronoun "lakum" (for you). According to the second interpretation, it is conjoined to "Ma'ayish." Some commentators have objected to the first interpretation, stating that a manifest noun in the genitive case cannot be conjoined to a genitive pronoun unless the preposition is repeated—meaning the "lam" should have been repeated before "man." Another objection is how the word "man" (who/those) is applied to non-human living creatures. However, neither objection is valid because the non-repetition of the preposition has precedents in Arabic, as does the application of "man" to non-rational beings. Conversely, the second interpretation can be criticized because "Ma'ayish" is such a broad concept that it already includes all means of life, including livestock; thus, there would be no need to mention them separately. For this reason, we preferred the first interpretation.

[^2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 9.

Therefore, it is not that Our power is limited or that We fear the exhaustion of provisions; rather, the source, treasury, and spring of everything is with Us, and We have the power to create any amount at any time. However, everything in this world follows a calculation, and provisions are sent down by Allah in a calculated measure.

Thus, we read elsewhere in the Quran: وَ لَوْ بَسَطَ اللَّهُ الرِّزْقَ لِعِبادِهِ لَبَغَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ لكِنْ يُنَزِّلُ بِقَدَرٍ ما يَشاءُ: "And if Allah had extended [excessively] provision for His servants, they would have committed tyranny throughout the earth. But He sends it down in an amount which He wills" (Ash-Shura: 27).

It is perfectly clear that striving for a living, besides keeping laziness, lethargy, and despondency away from humans and creating movement and vitality, is an excellent means for their healthy mental and physical engagement. If it were not so, and everything were available to man without measure, it is unknown what the world would look like.

A mass of idle people, with full stomachs and no control, would create an uproar. For we know that the people of this world are not like the people of Paradise, from whose hearts and souls every lust, selfishness, pride, and deviation has been washed away. Rather, they are humans with all sorts of good and bad traits who must be placed in the furnace of this world to be tempered, and what better than healthy effort, movement, and occupation to temper them?

Therefore, just as poverty and need drive man toward deviation and misery, excessive wealth is also a source of corruption and ruin.

Key Points

1. What are the Treasuries of Allah?

In several verses of the Quran, we read that Allah has treasuries; the treasuries of the heavens and the earth belong to Allah, or the treasuries of everything are with Him.

"Khaza'in" (خزائن) is the plural of "Khazanah" (خزانة), meaning a place where a person collects his property for safekeeping. It originally comes from the root khazn (to preserve and keep something). Naturally, one resorts to collecting, hoarding, and preserving something when his power is limited and he cannot provide whatever he wants at any time; thus, when he is able, he hoards what he deems necessary for a time of need and gathers it in a treasury.

But are these concepts conceivable regarding Allah? Certainly not. For this reason, a group of commentators, such as Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Fakhr Razi in Tafsir al-Kabir, and Raghib in Mufradat, have interpreted "the treasuries of Allah" to mean "Divine Decrees" (the power of Allah). That is, everything is gathered in the treasury of Allah’s power, and He creates whatever amount He deems necessary and wise.

However, some other great commentators have said that "the treasuries of Allah" refers to the collection of all matters that exist in the realm of existence and the material world, including the elements and the causes of their creation. In this collection, everything exists in abundance, but each of the specific products and beings of this world is created in a limited measure, without the possibility of existence being restricted only to that. [^1] Although this interpretation is acceptable in principle, the expression "with Us" (عندنا) is more consistent with the first interpretation.

In any case, the choice of expressions like "the treasuries of Allah," even though they do not apply to Allah in their common meaning, is because Allah wishes to speak to the people in their own language.

From what we have said, it also becomes clear that the interpretation of some commentators who limit "treasuries" specifically to "water and rain" is not only without evidence but also inconsistent with the breadth of the verse's concept.

2. Status-based Descent vs. Spatial Descent

As we have previously pointed out, "descent" (nuzul) does not always mean spatial descent—that is, coming from a higher place to a lower one. Rather, it sometimes means a descent in status. For example, whenever a blessing reaches subordinates from a great person, it is referred to as "descent." For this reason, in the Holy Quran, this word is used for Allah’s blessings, whether they descend from the sky, like rain, or are nurtured in the earth, like animals—as we read in Surah Az-Zumar, verse 6: وَ أَنْزَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَنْعامِ ثَمانِيَةَ أَزْواجٍ ("and He sent down for you eight pairs of livestock")—and regarding iron in Surah Al-Hadid, verse 25: وَ أَنْزَلْنَا الْحَدِيدَ ("and We sent down iron"), and the like.

In short, "Nuzul" and "Inzal" here mean existence, creation, and origination; however, because it originates from Allah toward His servants, such an expression is used.

[^1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 12, p. 148.

Ayahs

وَأَرْسَلْنَا ٱلرِّيَـٰحَ لَوَٰقِحَ فَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَسْقَيْنَـٰكُمُوهُ وَمَآ أَنتُمْ لَهُۥ بِخَـٰزِنِينَ (٢٢)
And We send the fertilizing winds and send down water from the sky providing it for you to drink and you are not maintainers of its resources. (22)
وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ نُحْىِۦ وَنُمِيتُ وَنَحْنُ ٱلْوَٰرِثُونَ (٢٣)
Indeed it is We who give life and bring death and We are the inheritors. (23)
وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْنَا ٱلْمُسْتَقْدِمِينَ مِنكُمْ وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْنَا ٱلْمُسْتَـْٔخِرِينَ (٢٤)
Certainly We know the predecessors among you and certainly We know the successors, (24)
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٥)
and indeed it is your Lord who will resurrect them. Indeed, He is all-wise, all-knowing. (25)

Commentary

The Role of Wind and Rain

Following the explanation of some of the secrets of creation and Allah’s blessings in the previous verses—such as the creation of the earth, mountains, plants, and the means of life—the first verse under discussion points to the blowing of winds and their effective role in the falling of rain, stating: "And We have sent the winds as fertilizing" (joining pieces of clouds together and making them fruitful) (وَ أَرْسَلْنَا الرِّياحَ لَواقِحَ‌).

"And thereafter, We sent down water from the sky" (فَأَنْزَلْنا مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً).

"And We gave it to you to drink" (فَأَسْقَيْناكُمُوهُ‌).

"While you were not the ones to store it" (وَ ما أَنْتُمْ لَهُ بِخازِنِينَ‌).

"Lawaqih" (لواقح) is the plural of "Laqih" (لاقح), meaning "fertilizer" or "impregnator." Here, it refers to the winds that join pieces of clouds together and prepare them for rain.

Although some contemporary scholars have sought to interpret this verse as an allusion to the pollination of plants by winds and pollen dispersal—thereby considering it one of the scientific matters that were not noted by human societies at the time of the Quran's revelation and counting it among the miracles of the Quran's scientific insights—one cannot interpret the above verse solely as such, despite accepting the reality that the blowing of winds has a profound effect on moving male pollen and fertilizing plants.

This is because immediately after this word, the descent of rain from the sky is mentioned (specifically with the conjunction "fa" indicating sequence/result), which shows that the "fertilizing" action of the winds is a preliminary step for the falling of rain.

In any case, the above expression is one of the most beautiful descriptions that can be said regarding clouds and the birth of rain from them. The clouds are likened to mothers and fathers who, with the help of the winds, unite, become pregnant, and deliver their offspring—the raindrops—to the earth.

The phrase: "While you were not the ones to store it" (وَ ما أَنْتُمْ لَهُ بِخازِنِينَ‌) may refer to the storage of rainwater before its descent; meaning, you cannot control these clouds, which are the primary sources of rain. It may also refer to the storage of rain after its descent; meaning, even after the rain falls, you are unable to collect and preserve it in vast quantities. It is Allah Who collects and stores it by freezing it on mountain peaks in the form of snow and ice, or by sending it into the depths of the earth to later appear as springs, qanats, and wells.

Then, following the discussions on Monotheism (Tawhid), it points to the Resurrection (Ma'ad) and its preliminaries, saying: "It is We Who give life and We Who cause death" (وَ إِنَّا لَنَحْنُ نُحْيِي وَ نُمِيتُ‌).

"And We are the Inheritors [of all]" (وَ نَحْنُ الْوارِثُونَ‌).

The reference to the issue of life and death—which are indeed the most important and certain matters—can serve both as a prelude to the discussion of Resurrection and as a completion of the discussion on Monotheism. This is because the phenomenon of life is the most wondrous phenomenon in the universe, and studying it can well acquaint us with the Creator of life. Fundamentally, the system of life and death is a system that is impossible without reliance on an Infinite Power and Knowledge.

On the other hand, the existence of life and death is itself proof that the beings of this world possess nothing of their own; whatever they have is from another source, and ultimately, Allah is the Inheritor of them all!

Then it adds: "And We surely know those who went before you and We surely know those who follow" (وَ لَقَدْ عَلِمْنَا الْمُسْتَقْدِمِينَ مِنْكُمْ وَ لَقَدْ عَلِمْنَا الْمُسْتَأْخِرِينَ‌). Therefore, both they themselves and their deeds are clear and manifest before Our knowledge, and from this perspective, the Resurrection, the rising, and the accounting of all their deeds are entirely easy for Us.

Hence, immediately following this statement, it says: "And indeed, your Lord is He Who will gather them all together [for the new life on the Day of Resurrection]" (وَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ‌). "For He is All-Wise, All-Knowing" (إِنَّهُ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ‌).

His "Wisdom" (Hikmah) necessitates that death is not the end of everything. For if life were limited to these few days of existence in this world, the creation of the universe would be vain and hollow, and it is far from the Wise Allah to have such a fruitless creation.

However, when this creation is a prelude to preparation for a permanent journey toward infinity—or in other words, a prelude to an eternal and everlasting life—it becomes fully meaningful and consistent with His Wisdom. For the Wise One does nothing without purpose.

And His being "All-Knowing" (Alim) ensures that no problem arises in the matter of Resurrection and the Gathering. He collects every particle of dust belonging to a human that has been scattered to any corner and grants it new life. Furthermore, the record of everyone's deeds is recorded both in the heart of this natural world and within the souls of humans, and He is aware of all of it.

Therefore, Allah being All-Wise and All-Knowing is considered a concise and profound proof for the issue of the Gathering and Resurrection.

Key Points Who are the Predecessors and the Successors?

In the interpretation of the verse: "And We surely know those who went before you and We surely know those who follow" (وَ لَقَدْ عَلِمْنَا الْمُسْتَقْدِمِينَ مِنْكُمْ وَ لَقَدْ عَلِمْنَا الْمُسْتَأْخِرِينَ‌), commentators have offered many possibilities. The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan mentions six interpretations, Qurtubi mentions eight, and Abu al-Futuh Razi mentions about ten possibilities. However, a careful examination shows that all of them can be combined into a single interpretation because:

The words "Mustaqdimin" (those who go before) and "Musta'khirin" (those who follow) have a broad meaning that includes those who are ahead or behind in time, as well as those who are foremost in good deeds, or in Jihad and the struggle against the enemies of truth, or even in the rows of congregational prayer, and the like.

Given this comprehensive meaning, all the possibilities suggested for "precedence" and "delay" in the above verse can be integrated and accepted.

In a Hadith, we read that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله emphasized greatly the participation in the first row of congregational prayer, saying: "Allah and the angels send blessings upon those who are foremost in these rows." Following this emphasis, people crowded to participate in the first row. There was a tribe called "Bani Udrah" whose houses were far from the mosque; they said, "We will sell our houses and buy houses near the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله mosque so we can reach the first row." The above verse was revealed (reminding them that Allah knows your intentions; even if you are in the last row, since you intend to be in the first row, you will have the reward of your intention) [1]. It is certain that the specificity of this occasion of revelation (Shan al-Nuzul) never limits the broad concept of the verse.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse discussed.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِن صَلْصَـٰلٍ مِّنْ حَمَإٍ مَّسْنُونٍ (٢٦)
Certainly We created man out of a dry clay [drawn] from an aging mud, (26)
وَٱلْجَآنَّ خَلَقْنَـٰهُ مِن قَبْلُ مِن نَّارِ ٱلسَّمُومِ (٢٧)
and We created the jinn earlier, out of a piercing fire. (27)
وَإِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ إِنِّى خَـٰلِقٌۢ بَشَرًا مِّن صَلْصَـٰلٍ مِّنْ حَمَإٍ مَّسْنُونٍ (٢٨)
When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to create a human out of a dry clay [drawn] from an aging mud. (28)
فَإِذَا سَوَّيْتُهُۥ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِى فَقَعُوا۟ لَهُۥ سَـٰجِدِينَ (٢٩)
So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My spirit, then fall down in prostration before him.’ (29)
فَسَجَدَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ (٣٠)
Thereat the angels prostrated, all of them together, (30)
إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ أَبَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونَ مَعَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ (٣١)
but not Iblis: he refused to be among those who prostrated. (31)
قَالَ يَـٰٓإِبْلِيسُ مَا لَكَ أَلَّا تَكُونَ مَعَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ (٣٢)
He said, ‘O Iblis! What kept you from being among those who have prostrated?’ (32)
قَالَ لَمْ أَكُن لِّأَسْجُدَ لِبَشَرٍ خَلَقْتَهُۥ مِن صَلْصَـٰلٍ مِّنْ حَمَإٍ مَّسْنُونٍ (٣٣)
Said he, ‘I will not prostrate before a human whom You have created out of a dry clay [drawn] from an aging mud.’ (33)
قَالَ فَٱخْرُجْ مِنْهَا فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ (٣٤)
He said, ‘Begone hence, for you are indeed an outcast, (34)
وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ ٱللَّعْنَةَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ (٣٥)
and indeed the curse shall lie on you until the Day of Retribution.’ (35)
قَالَ رَبِّ فَأَنظِرْنِىٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ (٣٦)
He said, ‘My Lord! Respite me till the day they will be resurrected.’ (36)
قَالَ فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنظَرِينَ (٣٧)
Said He, ‘You are indeed among the reprieved (37)
إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْوَقْتِ ٱلْمَعْلُومِ (٣٨)
until the day of the known time.’ (38)
قَالَ رَبِّ بِمَآ أَغْوَيْتَنِى لَأُزَيِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٣٩)
He said, ‘My Lord! As You have consigned me to perversity, I will surely glamorize [evil] for them on the earth, and I will surely pervert them, all (39)
إِلَّا عِبَادَكَ مِنْهُمُ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ (٤٠)
except Your dedicated servants among them.’ (40)
قَالَ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ عَلَىَّ مُسْتَقِيمٌ (٤١)
He said, ‘This is the path [leading] straight to Me. (41)
إِنَّ عِبَادِى لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَـٰنٌ إِلَّا مَنِ ٱتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ ٱلْغَاوِينَ (٤٢)
Indeed, as for My servants you do not have any authority over them, except the perverse who follow you, (42)
وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمَوْعِدُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٤٣)
and indeed hell is the tryst of them all. (43)
لَهَا سَبْعَةُ أَبْوَٰبٍ لِّكُلِّ بَابٍ مِّنْهُمْ جُزْءٌ مَّقْسُومٌ (٤٤)
It has seven gates, and to each gate belongs a separate portion of them.’ (44)

Commentary

The Creation of Man

Following the previous verses which described parts of Allah’s creation and the system of existence, these verses address the great masterpiece of creation—the creation of man. Through several profound verses, it recounts many details of this creation. Here, we first provide a brief commentary on the verses and then discuss the key points separately.

First, He says: "We created man from 'Salṣāl' (dry clay that makes a sound when struck) which was derived from 'Ḥama’ Masnūn' (dark, altered, and odorous mud)" (وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسَانَ مِنْ صَلْصَالٍ مِنْ حَمَإٍ مَسْنُونٍ).

"And the race of 'Jinn,' We created before that from a piercing and scorching fire" (وَالْجَانَّ خَلَقْنَاهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ مِنْ نَارِ السَّمُومِ). "Samūm" literally refers to a scorching wind that seems to penetrate all the pores of the human skin. This is because the Arabs call the tiny pores of the skin masāmm, and such a wind is called samūm for this reason. The word for poison (samm) is also derived from the same root because it penetrates the body and kills or sickens a person.

The discourse returns to the creation of man, describing Allah’s conversation with the angels that took place before man’s creation: "And remember when your Lord said to the angels: I am creating a human being from dry clay derived from dark, odorous mud" (وَإِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ إِنِّي خَالِقٌ بَشَرًا مِنْ صَلْصَالٍ مِنْ حَمَإٍ مَسْنُونٍ).

"So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My spirit (a noble, pure, and magnificent spirit), fall down in prostration to him" (فَإِذَا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي فَقَعُوا لَهُ سَاجِدِينَ). The creation of man reached its completion; whatever was worthy of the human body and soul was granted to him, and everything was finalized: "At that moment, all the angels prostrated, every one of them" (فَسَجَدَ الْمَلَائِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ).

The only one who did not obey this command was "Iblis"; therefore, it adds: "Except Iblis, who refused to be among those who prostrated" (إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ أَبَى أَنْ يَكُونَ مَعَ السَّاجِدِينَ).

It was here that Iblis was interrogated, and Allah "said to him: O Iblis, what is the matter with you that you are not with those who prostrate?" (قَالَ يَا إِبْلِيسُ مَا لَكَ أَلَّا تَكُونَ مَعَ السَّاجِدِينَ).

Iblis, drowned in his own pride and selfishness to the extent that it had clouded his intellect and reason, insolently replied to the Lord’s question and "said: I will never prostrate to a human whom You created from dry clay derived from odorous mud" (قَالَ لَمْ أَكُنْ لِأَسْجُدَ لِبَشَرٍ خَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ صَلْصَالٍ مِنْ حَمَإٍ مَسْنُونٍ).

Where is the luminous and flaming fire, and where is the dark and foul-smelling earth? Should a noble being like me humble himself before an inferior being?! What kind of law is this?!

Because of his selfishness and pride, he remained ignorant of the secrets of creation and forgot the blessings of the earth—which is the source of every good and blessing. More importantly, he ignored that noble and great Divine spirit that existed within Adam. Suddenly, he fell from the height of the station he held; he was no longer worthy of being in the ranks of the angels. Therefore, immediately "Allah said to him: Get out of it (from Paradise, or the heavens, or the ranks of the angels), for you are Rajīm (rejected and expelled from Our presence)" (قَالَ فَاخْرُجْ مِنْهَا فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ).

"And know that this pride of yours became the cause of your disbelief, and this disbelief has made you an outcast forever; 'And indeed, the curse and distance from Allah’s mercy shall be upon you until the Day of Judgment!'" (وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ اللَّعْنَةَ إِلَى يَوْمِ الدِّينِ).

At this moment, Iblis, seeing himself expelled from Allah’s presence and feeling that the creation of man was the cause of his misery, felt the fire of malice ignite in his heart. He sought to take revenge on the children of Adam, even though the primary culprit was himself—not Adam, nor Allah’s command. However, his pride and selfishness, coupled with his stubbornness, did not allow him to perceive this reality.

Therefore, "he said: My Lord, since it is so, give me respite until the Day they are resurrected!" (قَالَ رَبِّ فَأَنْظِرْنِي إِلَى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ). Not so that he might repent, or be sorry for what he had done and seek to make amends, but rather to continue his stubbornness, enmity, and defiance!

Allah accepted this request of his and "said: Indeed, you are of those given respite" (قَالَ فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ الْمُنْظَرِينَ).

But not until the day the creation is resurrected as he had requested, but rather "until the time of the well-known day" (إِلَى يَوْمِ الْوَقْتِ الْمَعْلُومِ). Regarding what is meant by "the well-known day" (يَوْمِ الْوَقْتِ الْمَعْلُومِ), commentators have offered several possibilities:

Some have said it refers to the end of this world and the termination of the period of religious obligation, because after that, according to the apparent meaning of Quranic verses, all inhabitants of the world will perish and only the Pure Essence of Allah will remain. Thus, only a portion of Iblis's request was granted.

Others have suggested that "the well-known time" refers to a specific time known only to Allah, and no one else is aware of it; for if He had revealed that time, Iblis would have been encouraged to commit more sin and rebellion.

Some have also suggested it refers to the Day of Resurrection, as he wanted to remain alive until that day to enjoy eternal life, and his request was granted—especially since the expression "the well-known day" (يَوْمِ الْوَقْتِ الْمَعْلُومِ) appears in verse 50 of Surah Al-Waqi'ah in reference to the Day of Resurrection.

However, this last possibility is very unlikely, because if that were the case, Allah would have fully granted his request, whereas the appearance of the above verses suggests that his request was not fully granted, but only until "the well-known day."

In any case, the first interpretation is most consistent with the spirit and appearance of the verse, and this meaning is explicitly stated in some narrations transmitted from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام [1].

At this point, Iblis revealed his inner intention, though nothing was hidden from Allah, and "he said: My Lord! Because You have led me astray (and this human provided the grounds for my misery), I will surely adorn for them material blessings on the earth, and I will keep humans occupied with them, and eventually, I will lead them all astray" (قَالَ رَبِّ بِمَا أَغْوَيْتَنِي لَأُزَيِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ).

However, he knew well that his whisperings would never affect the hearts of the "sincere servants of Allah," and his traps would not occupy them. In short, the pure and sincere servants are so powerful that they break the chains of Satan's captivity. Therefore, he immediately made an exception to his statement and said: "Except Your chosen (sincere) servants among them" (إِلَّا عِبَادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ). It is obvious that Allah never led Satan astray; rather, this was a satanic attribution made by Iblis to supposedly exonerate himself and provide a justification for his misleading others. This is the custom of all Iblises and satans:

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 13, Hadith 45.

firstly, they cast their sins upon others, and secondly, they strive everywhere to justify their ugly deeds with false logic—not only before the servants of Allah but even before Allah Himself, who is aware of all things.

Additionally, it should be noted that "Mukhlaṣīn" is the plural of "Mukhlaṣ" (with a fatḥah on the lām). As we explained in the commentary of Surah Yusuf, it refers to someone who has reached the highest stage of faith and action after education, training, and struggling against the self (nafs), becoming impenetrable to the whisperings of Satan and every tempter [1].

To humiliate Satan and strengthen the hearts of the seekers of the right path and the travelers of the way of Monotheism, Allah "said: This is a straight path [binding] upon Me..." and this is an undying tradition that I have taken upon Myself... (قَالَ هَذَا صِرَاطٌ عَلَيَّ مُسْتَقِيمٌ).

That "Indeed, My servants—you have no authority or power over them, except the deviants who personally choose to follow you" (إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغَاوِينَ). This means that, in reality, it is not you who can lead people astray; rather, it is the deviant humans who, by their own will and desire, answer your call and walk behind you.

In other words, this verse is an indication of the freedom of human will, and that Iblis and his army can never forcibly drag anyone to the path of corruption. Rather, it is the humans themselves who respond to his call, open the doors of their hearts to him, and give him permission to enter!

[1]: Refer to Volume 9 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 377.

In summary, although Satan's whisperings are effective, the final decision does not lie with Satan; it lies with the humans themselves, for man can stand against his whisperings and reject him. In fact, Allah wants to remove this vain thought and raw imagination from Satan's mind—that he would gain undisputed rule over humans.

Then, He directs His most explicit threats toward the followers of Satan, saying: "And indeed, Hell is the promised place for them all" (وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمَوْعِدُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ). Let them not think they can escape the clutches of punishment or that their account will not be settled; the accounts of all of them will be settled together in one place.

"That very Hell which has seven gates, and for every gate, a group of Satan's followers is assigned" (لَهَا سَبْعَةُ أَبْوَابٍ لِكُلِّ بَابٍ مِنْهُمْ جُزْءٌ مَقْسُومٌ). These gates are, in truth, the gates of sins through which individuals enter Hell. Each group enters through a gate by committing a particular sin, just as the gates of Paradise are the acts of obedience, righteous deeds, and struggles through which the people of Paradise enter it.

Key Points

1- Arrogance and Pride are the Source of the Greatest Miseries

The most important educational point derived from the story of Iblis and the creation of Adam, which appears in various Surahs of the Quran, is the terrifying fall of Iblis from that high station he held due to arrogance and pride! We know that Iblis was not of the angels (as derived from verse 50 of Surah Al-Kahf), and he had attained such an elevation of status under the shadow of obeying Allah's command that he was in the ranks of the angels. Some even say he was the teacher of the angels, and according to what is derived from the Qasi'ah sermon in Nahj al-Balagha, he had worshipped Allah for thousands of years.

However, he lost all these positions because of one hour of arrogance and pride. He became so entangled in prejudice and self-worship that he did not even seek to apologize or repent; instead, he continued his course and remained so steadfast on the path of stubbornness that he decided to accept the responsibility of participating in the crimes of all the oppressors and sinners among the children of Adam as a tempter, and to endure the equivalent of the punishment and torment of all of them at once! This is the result of selfishness, pride, prejudice, self-conceit, and arrogance.

It is not only Iblis; we have seen with our own eyes satanic humans, or read their biographies in the black pages of history, who, when they mounted the steed of pride, arrogance, and selfishness, dragged a world into dust and blood. It is as if a veil of blood and ignorance disabled their physical and inner eyes, and they perceive no truth, walking madly on the path of oppression and injustice, and finally falling into the worst of precipices.

This arrogance and pride is a scorching and terrifying fire. Just as a person might toil for many years to provide a house, tools, and capital for life, but turn the product of it into ashes in a few moments with just one flame of fire, it is also entirely possible to lose the product of thousands of years of obedience with one hour of arrogance and pride before Allah. What lesson is more eloquent and shaking than this?!

The wonder is that he did not even pay attention to this clear point: that fire is not superior to earth. For the source of all blessings—plants, animals, minerals, the storage place of waters—in short, the source of the emergence of every living being is the earth. But the function of fire is burning and, in many cases, destruction.

Ali عليه السلام, in that same Qasi'ah sermon (Sermon 192 of Nahj al-Balagha), refers to Iblis as the "Enemy of Allah" (Aduww Allah), the "Imam of the prejudiced" (Imam al-Muta'assibin), and the "Predecessor of the arrogant" (Salaf al-Mustakbirin), saying: "For this reason, Allah stripped the garment of honor from his body and cast the veil of humiliation over his head." Then he adds: "Do you not see how Allah belittled him for his arrogance? And lowered him for his self-exaltation? He was expelled in this world, and a painful punishment was prepared for him in the hereafter" (Ala tarawna kayfa saghgharahu Allahu bi-takabburihi, wa wada'ahu bi-taraffu'ihi, fa-ja'alahu fi al-dunya madhuran wa a'adda lahu fi al-akhirati sa'ira).

Additionally, as we mentioned, Iblis was the first founder of the school of fatalism (Jabr)—that school which is contrary to the conscience of every human being, and one of the major reasons for its emergence is to exonerate sinful humans for their actions. In the above verses, we read that Iblis, to exonerate himself and prove that he has the right to strive in misleading the children of Adam, resorted to this same big lie and said: "O Allah, You led me astray, and for this reason, I will lead the children of Adam astray, except the sincere ones!"

2- Over Whom Does Satan Gain Authority?

Again, we find it necessary to mention that the influence of satanic whisperings in man is not an unconscious or forced influence. Rather, we admit his whispering into our hearts by our own will; otherwise, even Satan himself knows that he has no way over the sincere ones—those who have purified themselves under the light of training and have wiped the rust of polytheism from their souls and spirits.

In a more expressive term—the same term used in the above verses—the relationship between Satan and the misguided is the relationship of "leader" and "follower," not the relationship of "compeller" and "compelled."

3- The Gates of Hell!

In the above verses, we read that Hell has seven gates (it is not unlikely that the number seven here is a number of multiplicity, meaning it has many and numerous gates, just as the number seven is used in this sense in verse 27 of Surah Luqman).

However, it is clear that this multiplicity of gates (like the multiplicity of the gates of Paradise) is neither because of the large number of entrants such that they cannot enter through one small gate, nor is it for ceremonial purposes. Rather, it is in truth an indication of the various factors that drag a person to Hell; every type of sin is considered a gate.

In the Sermon of Jihad in Nahj al-Balagha, we read: Inna al-jihada babun min abwabi al-jannah fatahahullahu li-khasati awliya'ih: "Indeed, Jihad is one of the gates of Paradise which Allah has opened for His special elite servants" [1]. And in a famous hadith, we read: Inna al-suyufa maqalidu al-jannah: "Indeed, swords are the keys to Paradise." Such expressions clearly clarify the meaning of the multiple gates of Paradise and Hell.

It is noteworthy that in a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "Paradise has eight gates" [1], while the above verses say: "Hell has seven gates." This difference indicates that although the gates to misery and punishment are many, the gates to reaching happiness and bliss are even more numerous (we also discussed this under verse 23 of Surah al-Ra'd).

4- "Dark Mud" and "The Spirit of Allah"

Interestingly, it is clearly derived from these verses that man was created from two different things: one at the highest level of greatness, and the other apparently at the lowest level in terms of value.

The material aspect of man is formed by dark, foul-smelling mud (slime), and his spiritual aspect is something referred to as the "Spirit of Allah."

Of course, Allah has neither a body nor a spirit, and the attribution of the spirit to Allah is what is called a "noble attribution" (idafah tashrifiyyah). it is proof that a very magnificent spirit was breathed into the human frame, just as the House of the Kaaba is called "The House of Allah" (Bayt Allah) because of its greatness, and the holy month of Ramadan is called "The Month of Allah" (Shahr Allah) because of its blessing.

For this reason, the ascending arc of this human is so high that it reaches a place where he sees nothing but Allah, and his descending arc is so low that he becomes lower than the cattle (bal hum adall). This vast distance between the "ascending arc" and the "descending arc" is itself proof of the extraordinary importance of this creature.

Also, this special composition is proof that the greatness of man's station is not because of his material aspect, for if we return to his material aspect, he is nothing more than slime.

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 27. [1]: Khisal of al-Saduq, Abwab al-Thamaniyah.

It is this Divine spirit, with the extraordinary talents hidden within it that can be the manifestation of Allah's lights, that has granted him all this greatness. The only way for his perfection is to strengthen it and use the material aspect—which is a means for this very goal—in the path of advancing this purpose (since it can be an effective aid in reaching that great goal).

From the verses of Adam's creation at the beginning of Surah al-Baqarah, it is also derived that the angels' prostration before Adam was because of his special Divine knowledge. As for the question of how prostration to other than Allah is possible, and whether the angels truly prostrated to Allah because of this wondrous creation or whether their prostration was for Adam, we explained the answer under those same verses related to Adam's creation in Surah al-Baqarah (refer to Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 127).

5- What is the "Jann"?

The word "Jinn" originally means something that is hidden from human senses. For example, we say jannat al-layl—or—falamma janna 'alayhi al-layl, meaning when the black veil of night covered him. For this reason, majnun refers to someone whose intellect is covered, janin to an infant covered in the mother's womb, jannat to a garden whose ground is covered by trees, janan to the heart covered within the chest, and junnah meaning a shield that covers a person from the enemy's strikes.

Of course, it is derived from the verses of the Quran that the "Jinn" are a type of rational being hidden from human senses, and their creation is originally from fire or the smokeless flames of fire, and Iblis is also from this group.

Some scholars describe them as a type of "rational spirits" that are abstract from matter (of course, it is clear they do not have complete abstraction because something created from matter is material, but they have a semi-abstraction because they are not perceived by our senses; in other words, they are a type of subtle body).

It also appears from the verses of the Quran that they have believers and disbelievers, obedient and rebellious ones, and they also have religious obligations and responsibilities.

Of course, the explanation of these matters, as well as their harmony with modern science, requires more discussion, which we will—Allah willing—discuss in the commentary of Surah al-Jinn in the 29th part of the Quran.

A point that is necessary to mention here is that in the above verses, the word "Jann" appears, which is from the same root as "Jinn."

Are these two words (Jinn and Jann) both of the same meaning, or as some commentators have said, is the Jann a specific type of Jinn?

If we place the Quranic verses that have appeared in this regard side by side, it becomes clear that both are of the same meaning, because in the Quran, sometimes "Jinn" is placed in contrast to man, and sometimes "Jann" is placed in contrast to man.

For example, in verse 88 of Surah al-Isra, we read: qul la'in ijtama'ati al-insu wa al-jinn, and in verse 56 of Surah al-Dhariyat, we read: wa ma khalaqtu al-jinna wa al-insa illa li-ya'budun.

While in verse 15 of Surah al-Rahman, we read: khalaqa al-insana min salsalin kal-fakhkhar wa khalaqa al-janna min marijin min nar, and again in the same Surah, verse 39, we read: fa-yawma'idhin la yus'alu 'an dhanbihi insun wa la jann.

From the sum of the above verses and other verses of the Quran, it is well understood that Jann and Jinn are both of the same meaning, and therefore in the above verses, sometimes "Jinn" is placed against man and sometimes "Jann."

Of course, "Jann" has appeared in another meaning in the Quran as well, which is a type of snake, as we read in the story of Moses: ka'annaha jann (Al-Qasas: 31), but that is outside our discussion.

6- The Quran and the Creation of Man

As we saw in the above verses, the Quran has a condensed discussion regarding the creation of man, which it has passed over somewhat briefly and generally because the main purpose was educational matters. Similar discussions appear in several other places in the Quran, such as Surah al-Sajdah, al-Mu'minun, Surah Sad, and others.

Of course, we know that the Quran is not a book of natural sciences, but rather a book of human-building. Therefore, one should not expect the details of these sciences—such as matters related to evolution, anatomy, embryology, botany, and the like—to be raised in the Quran. However, this does not prevent short references to parts of these sciences from being made in the Quran in accordance with educational discussions.

In any case, after noting this short introduction, we have two discussions here that seem necessary to raise:

1- The evolution of species from a scientific perspective. 2- The evolution of species from the perspective of the Quran.

First, we go to the first discussion and, excluding verses and narrations and relying only on the specific criteria of natural sciences, we discuss this issue:

We know that among natural scientists, two hypotheses have existed regarding the creation of living beings, including plants and animals:

A: The hypothesis of the evolution of species or "Transformism," which says that types of living beings were not in their current form at the beginning. Rather, the beginning of single-celled organisms occurred in the waters of the oceans and from the slime of the deep seas with a mutation; that is, inanimate matter was placed in special conditions from which the first living cells emerged.

These microscopic living beings gradually evolved and changed form from one type to another, moving from the seas to the deserts and from there to the air, and types of plants and types of aquatic and land animals and birds came into existence.

The most complete link of this evolution is these humans of today, who appeared from beings similar to monkeys, and then humanoid monkeys.

B: The hypothesis of the fixity of species or "Fixism," which says that types of living beings each appeared separately from the beginning in this current form, and no type has transformed into another type, and naturally, man also had an independent creation, having been created in this very form from the beginning.

Scientists of both groups have written extensively to prove their belief, and many wars and disputes have broken out in scientific circles over this issue. These wars intensified from the time of Lamarck (the famous French zoologist who lived in the late 18th and early 19th centuries) and then Darwin, the English zoologist who lived in the 19th century, who presented their views on the evolution of species with new evidence.

However, in natural science circles today, there is no doubt that the majority of scientists are supporters of the theory of evolution.

Arguments of the Proponents of Evolution

Their arguments can easily be summarized into three parts:

First are the arguments they have brought from paleontology and the study of fossils—that is, the petrified skeletons of past living beings. They believe that studies of the different layers of the earth show that living beings have changed form from simpler forms to more complete and complex forms. The only way the difference and variation in fossils can be interpreted is through this hypothesis of evolution.

Another argument is the evidence they have gathered from "comparative anatomy." Through detailed and lengthy discussions, they say that when we dissect the skeletal structures of different animals and compare them, we see a great similarity in them, which shows they were all taken from one origin.

Finally, their third argument is the evidence they have obtained from "embryology." They believe that if we place animals in the embryonic state—where they have not yet achieved the necessary development—side by side, we will see to what extent the embryos resemble each other before development in the mother's womb or inside the egg. This also confirms that they were all taken from one origin at the beginning.

Responses of the Proponents of the Fixity of Species

However, the proponents of the hypothesis of the fixity of species have a general response to all these arguments: that none of this evidence is convincing. Of course, it cannot be denied that each of these three pieces of evidence justifies the probability of evolution in the mind as a "conjectural probability," but it will never bring certainty.

To put it more clearly, the proof of the hypothesis of evolution and its transformation from a hypothesis into a scientific and definitive law must either be through rational proof or through testing, sense, and experience; and there is no way other than these two.

But on one hand, we know that rational and philosophical arguments have no way into these matters, and on the other hand, the hand of experience and testing is short of matters whose roots lie millions of years ago!

What we perceive with sense and experience is that superficial changes occur over time in the form of "mutations" in animals and plants. For example, from the generation of ordinary sheep, a sheep is suddenly born whose wool is different from that of ordinary sheep—meaning it is much finer and softer—and that becomes the source of the emergence of a generation of sheep called "Merino sheep," with this characteristic in the wool.

Or that animals, due to mutation, develop a change in eye color or nails or the shape of the skin and the like.

But no one has yet seen a mutation that creates a major change in the main organs of an animal's body or transforms one type into another.

Therefore, we can only guess that the accumulation of mutations might one day lead to a change in the type of animal, and for example, transform reptiles into birds. But this guess is never a definitive guess; rather, it is only a conjectural matter because we have never encountered mutations that change main organs as a matter of sense and experience.

From the sum of what has been said, we conclude that the three arguments of the proponents of transformism cannot take this theory beyond the form of a hypothesis. For this reason, those who discuss these matters precisely always speak of it as the "hypothesis of the evolution of species," not a law or principle.

The Hypothesis of Evolution and the Issue of Theology

Although many strive to posit a kind of contradiction between this hypothesis and the issue of theology—and perhaps from one perspective they are right, because the emergence of the belief in Darwinism created a fierce war between the church authorities on one side and the proponents of this hypothesis on the other, and on this issue in that era, for political and social reasons that are not the place to explain here, extensive propaganda broke out that Darwinism is not compatible with theology.

But today this issue is clear to us: that these two do not contradict each other. That is, whether we accept the hypothesis of evolution or reject it due to lack of evidence, in both cases, we can be believers in God.

If the hypothesis of evolution were proven, it would take the form of a scientific law that unveils a natural cause and effect. There is no difference between this cause-and-effect relationship in the world of living beings and other beings. Would the discovery of the natural causes of rainfall, tides, earthquakes, and the like be an obstacle in the way of theology? Certainly not. Therefore, the discovery of an evolutionary relationship among types of beings also creates no obstacle in the path of knowing God.

Only those who imagined that the discovery of natural causes is inconsistent with accepting the existence of God can say such a thing. But today we know well that not only does the discovery of these causes not harm Monotheism, but it itself places new arguments from the system of creation before us to prove the existence of God.

Interestingly, Darwin himself stood up against the accusation of atheism and irreligion, and in his book (The Origin of Species), he explicitly states: "While accepting the evolution of species, I am a worshiper of God, and fundamentally, evolution cannot be justified without accepting the existence of God."

Pay attention to this expression: "Despite accepting natural causes for the emergence of different types of living beings, he always remained a believer in the One God, and as his age increased, a special inner feeling of perceiving a power beyond human intensified in him, to the extent that he found the enigma of creation insoluble for man" [1]. Fundamentally, he believed that the guidance and leadership of species in this wondrous labyrinth of evolution, and the transformation of a very simple living being into all these different and diverse types of animals, is not possible without the existence of a calculated and precise plan from a Universal Intellect! Truly it is so: is it possible to bring forth all these amazing and wondrous derivatives, each of which has its own detailed organization, from a single and very simple and lowly matter without relying on an infinite knowledge and power?

The result is: the clamor of the contradiction of the belief in the evolution of species with the issue of theology has been a baseless and groundless clamor (whether we accept the hypothesis of evolution or not).

Only this issue remains: whether the hypothesis of the evolution of species contradicts the history that the Quran mentions for the creation of Adam or not?! Which is discussed below.

The Quran and the Issue of Evolution

Interestingly, both the proponents of the evolution of species and its deniers—that is, those who have been among the Muslims—have resorted to Quranic verses to prove their aim. But perhaps both groups have sometimes, under the influence of their own belief, argued from verses that have little connection to their aim. Therefore, we select verses from both sides that are worthy of discussion and deliberation.

The most important verse that the proponents of evolution rely on is verse 33 of Surah Al-Imran: inna Allaha istafa Adama wa Nuhan wa ala Ibrahima wa ala Imrana 'ala al-'alamin: "Indeed, Allah chose Adam and Noah and the family of Abraham and the family of Imran over the worlds."

They say that just as Noah and the family of Abraham and the family of Imran lived among a nation and were chosen from among them, so too must Adam be. That is, in his era and time, humans who were given the name "worlds" ('alamin) must have existed, and Adam was Allah's chosen one from among them. This shows that Adam was not the first human on earth; rather, there were other humans before him, and Adam's distinction was his intellectual and spiritual mutation which became the cause of his being chosen from among his peers.

They have also mentioned several other verses, some of which have no connection at all to the issue of evolution, and interpreting them as evolution is more of the nature of interpretation by personal opinion (tafsir bi al-ra'y). Another part is compatible both with the evolution of species and with their fixity and the independent creation of Adam. For this reason, we saw it better to refrain from mentioning them.

But the objection that can be made to this argument is that if "worlds" ('alamin) meant contemporary people and "choosing" (istifa) must necessarily take place from among such persons, this argument would be acceptable. But if someone says "worlds" is more general than contemporaries and non-contemporaries—just as it is narrated in the famous hadith regarding the virtue of the Lady of Islam, Fatimah (peace be upon her), from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله where he says: "As for my daughter Fatimah, she is the leader of the women of the worlds from the first and the last"—in this case, the above verse will have no indication of this aim. It would be exactly as if someone said Allah chose a group from among humans (humans of all centuries and eras), one of whom is Adam. In this case, there is no necessity for other humans to have existed in the era and time of Adam who were called "worlds" or for Adam to have been chosen from among them.

Especially since the talk is about Allah's choosing—the God who was well aware of the future and the generations that would come in later times [1].

[1]: Darwinism written by Mahmoud Behzad, pages 75 and 76. [1]: There is also the possibility that Adam's children formed a small society in a not-so-long period, of which Adam was the chosen one.

And as for the most important argument that the proponents of the fixity of species have selected from the verses of the Quran, it is the verses under discussion and the like, which say: Allah created man from dry clay that was taken from dark, foul-smelling mud. Interestingly, this expression is used both regarding the creation of "man" (al-insan) (verse 26 of Al-Hijr) and regarding "human" (bashar) (verse 28 of Al-Hijr), and by the context of the mention of the angels' prostration after it, it refers to the person of Adam (pay attention to verses 29, 30, and 31 of Surah Al-Hijr which we brought above).

The appearance of these verses at first glance says that Adam was first created from dark mud, and after the completion of the frame, the Divine spirit was breathed into it, followed by the angels falling in prostration before him, except Iblis.

The manner of expression of these verses shows that between the creation of Adam from dust and the emergence of the current form, no other types existed.

And the use of "thumma" (then) which appears in some of the above verses and is used in the Arabic language for "sequence with a gap," is never proof of the passing of millions of years and the existence of thousands of types. Rather, there is no obstacle to it referring to the gaps that existed between the stages of Adam's creation from dust, then dry clay, and then the breathing of the Divine spirit.

Therefore, this same word "thumma" has appeared regarding the creation of man in the embryonic world and the stages he passes through one after another, such as: ya ayyuha al-nasu in kuntum fi raybin min al-ba'thi fa-inna khalaqnakum min turabin thumma min nutfatin thumma min 'alaqatin thumma min mudghatin... thumma nukhrijukum tiflan thumma li-tablughu ashuddakum: "O people, if you are in doubt about the Resurrection, [consider] that We created you from dust, then from a drop, then from a clinging clot, then from a lump of flesh... then We bring you out as a child, then [We develop you] that you may reach your maturity" (Surah Al-Hajj, verse 5).

You notice that there is no necessity for "thumma" to be for a long gap; rather, just as it is used for long gaps, it is also used for short gaps.

From the sum of what we said above, we conclude that although the Quranic verses are not directly seeking to state the issue of evolution or the fixity of species, the apparent meanings of the verses (of course, specifically regarding man) are more compatible with the issue of independent creation. Although it is not completely explicit, the appearance of the verses of Adam's creation mostly revolves around independent creation. However, regarding other living beings, the Quran is silent.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (٤٥)
Indeed the Godwary will be amid gardens and springs. (45)
ٱدْخُلُوهَا بِسَلَـٰمٍ ءَامِنِينَ (٤٦)
‘‘Enter it in peace and safety!’’ (46)
وَنَزَعْنَا مَا فِى صُدُورِهِم مِّنْ غِلٍّ إِخْوَٰنًا عَلَىٰ سُرُرٍ مُّتَقَـٰبِلِينَ (٤٧)
We will remove whatever rancour there is in their breasts; [intimate like] brothers, [they will be reclining] on couches, facing one another. (47)
لَا يَمَسُّهُمْ فِيهَا نَصَبٌ وَمَا هُم مِّنْهَا بِمُخْرَجِينَ (٤٨)
Therein neither weariness shall touch them, nor will they [ever] be expelled from it. (48)
۞ نَبِّئْ عِبَادِىٓ أَنِّىٓ أَنَا ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٤٩)
Inform my servants that I am indeed the All-forgiving, the All-merciful, (49)

and that My punishment is a painful punishment. (50)

Commentary

The Eightfold Blessings of Paradise

In the preceding verses, we saw how Allah described the fate of Satan, his allies, companions, and followers, opening the seven gates of Hell to them.

Following the Quranic method of utilizing "contrast and symmetry" for the purpose of education and upbringing, these verses speak of "Paradise and its inhabitants," mentioning material and spiritual, physical and psychological blessings.

In truth, eight great material and spiritual blessings—corresponding to the number of the gates of Paradise—are described in these verses.

1- At the beginning, it points to an important physical blessing, stating: "Indeed, the righteous will be amid gardens and springs" (إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَ عُيُونٍ).

It is noteworthy that among all attributes, "Taqwa" (piety/God-wariness) is emphasized—that very piety, restraint, commitment, and responsibility which encompasses all prominent human virtues.

The mention of "gardens" (jannat) and "springs" (uyun) in the plural form refers to the diverse gardens and the numerous, varied springs of Paradise, each of which creates a fresh pleasure and possesses a unique characteristic.

2 and 3- Then, it points to two important spiritual blessings, which are "peace" (salama) and "security" (amniyya)—peace from every kind of suffering and discomfort, and security from every kind of danger. It says: "The divine angels welcome them and say: Enter them in peace and security" (ادْخُلُوها بِسَلامٍ آمِنِينَ).

In the following verse, three other important spiritual blessings are explicitly stated:

4- "We shall strip away whatever rancor is in their breasts" (وَ نَزَعْنا ما فِي صُدُورِهِمْ مِنْ غِلٍّ) [^1]. We wash away and uproot every kind of envy, malice, enmity, and betrayal from their hearts.

5- "As brothers," where the closest bonds of affection prevail among them (إِخْواناً).

6- "Facing one another on couches" (عَلى‌ سُرُرٍ مُتَقابِلِينَ) [^2]. In their social gatherings, they are not plagued by the distressing formalities of this world; their assemblies have no "high" or "low" seating, and there is no sign of the painful class-based structures of this world. All are brothers, all facing one another in a single row—not one at the head of the assembly and another by the doorway.

Of course, this does not contradict the difference in their status regarding spiritual degrees; this refers to their social gatherings, otherwise, each possesses a rank specific to their own degree of piety and faith.

7- Then, referring to the seventh material and spiritual blessing, it says: "No fatigue shall touch them therein" (لا يَمَسُّهُمْ فِيها نَصَبٌ). It is not like the life of this world, where reaching a single day of comfort is preceded and followed by so much exhaustion that the very thought of it disturbs one's peace.

8- Likewise, the thought of annihilation, destruction, or the ending of blessings does not distress them, for "they shall never be expelled from them" (وَ ما هُمْ مِنْها بِمُخْرَجِينَ).

Now that the abundant and delightful blessings of Paradise have been described so effectively and placed entirely at the disposal of the righteous and the pious, it is possible that sinful and tainted individuals might fall into a shadow of grief and sorrow, wishing: "If only we too could attain a corner of all these bounties." Here, the Compassionate and Merciful Allah opens the gates of Paradise to them as well, but conditionally.

With a tone full of love and the most sublime tenderness, He addresses His Prophet, saying: "O My Prophet! Inform My servants that I am indeed the All-Forgiving, the Most Merciful" (نَبِّئْ عِبادِي أَنِّي أَنَا الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ).

The expression "My servants" (ibadi) is a delicate term that fills every human being with longing, and following it with the description of God as the All-Forgiving and Merciful brings this yearning to its peak.

However, since the Quran always prevents the abuse of the manifestations of Divine Mercy with jarring phrases indicative of His wrath and anger—so that the balance between fear and hope, which is the secret to perfection and upbringing, is maintained—it immediately adds: and tell My servants that "My punishment is indeed the painful punishment" (وَ أَنَّ عَذابِي هُوَ الْعَذابُ الْأَلِيمُ).

Key Points

1- The Gardens and Springs of Paradise

Although it is very difficult, or rather impossible, for us who are in this limited world to comprehend the dimensions of the blessings of Paradise—since the blessings of this world compared to those are almost like zero compared to an extraordinarily large number—this does not prevent us from sensing rays of it with our intellect and soul.

What is certain is that the blessings of Paradise are very diverse, and the term "gardens" (jannat) used in the above verses and many others, as well as the term "springs" (uyun), testifies to this reality.

Of course, in the Quran (in Surahs Al-Insan, Al-Rahman, Al-Dukhan, Muhammad, and others), various types of these springs are mentioned, and their diversity is visualized through brief indications, which perhaps represent the various types of good deeds and righteous actions in this world. God willing, we will refer to their details in the commentary of those Surahs.

2- Material and Spiritual Blessings

Contrary to what some imagine, the Quran does not everywhere promise people only the material blessings of Paradise; rather, it repeatedly speaks of spiritual blessings as well, of which the above verses are a clear example. Thus, the first welcome and glad tidings that the angels give to the inhabitants of Paradise upon entering this great center of bounty are the tidings of "peace" and "security."

The washing away of malice from the breasts, and of ugly traits such as envy and betrayal that destroy the spirit of brotherhood, as well as the removal of false ceremonial distinctions that disrupt the peace of mind and soul, are among the other spiritual blessings mentioned in the verses above.

It is also worth noting that "peace" and "security," which are placed at the beginning of the heavenly blessings, are the foundation of every other blessing; without these two, no blessing can be enjoyed. Even in this world, the beginning of all blessings is the blessing of health (peace) and security.

3- Malice and Envy are the Enemies of Brotherhood

It is interesting that after mentioning the blessing of peace and security in the above verses and before stating the blessing of brotherhood, the issue of uprooting various disruptive traits such as malice, envy, pride, and betrayal is mentioned, referred to by the word "ghill" (غل), which has a broad meaning.

In reality, if the human heart is not cleansed of this "ghill," neither the blessing of peace and security will be provided, nor brotherhood; rather, there will always be war and strife, always quarrels and conflict, followed by the severing of the bonds of brotherhood and the deprivation of peace and security.

4- The Perfect Reward

According to some commentators, a reward or recompense is complete only when four conditions are met:

It must be a significant benefit; it must be accompanied by honor; it must be free from any discomfort; and it must be eternal and everlasting.

In the above verses, regarding the blessings of Paradise, all four parts are mentioned.

The phrase "Indeed, the righteous will be amid gardens and springs" refers to the first part.

The phrase "Enter them in peace and security" is evidence of honor and glorification.

The phrase "We shall strip away whatever rancor is in their breasts, as brothers facing one another on couches" refers to the negation of any spiritual discomfort or harm, and the phrase "No fatigue shall touch them therein" refers to the negation of physical harms.

Finally, the phrase "they shall never be expelled from them" expresses the last condition, which is the permanence and eternity of these blessings. Thus, this reward will be complete from every aspect [^3].

5- Let Us Build Paradise in This World

What has been depicted in the above verses regarding the material and spiritual blessings of Paradise actually constitutes the principles of the important blessings of this world as well. It seems it also wants to make us understand that by providing these blessings in the life of this world, you can build a small paradise that is a sample of that great Paradise.

If we wash away malice and enmity from our breasts.

If we strengthen the principles of brotherhood.

If we remove unnecessary and divisive formalities from our lives and especially our social gatherings.

If we return peace and security to our society.

And if all people are given the assurance that no one will interfere with their honor, personality, social position, and legitimate interests, so that they find confidence in the permanence of their bounties.

On that day, a sample of Paradise will be before your eyes.

[^1]: "Ghill" (غل) originally means the secret penetration of something, and for this reason, envy, malice, and enmity that penetrate the human soul in a mysterious way are called "ghill." Therefore, "ghill" has a broad concept that includes many ugly and disruptive moral traits (for more explanation, refer to Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 52, footnote).

[^2]: "Surur" (سرر) is the plural of "sarir" (سرير), which originally means a throne or chair and the like, upon which people sit and hold joyful gatherings (note that "surur" [thrones] and "surur" [joy] are from the same root).

[^3]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 19, p. 193.

Ayahs

وَنَبِّئْهُمْ عَن ضَيْفِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ (٥١)
And inform them about the guests of Abraham, (51)
إِذْ دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ فَقَالُوا۟ سَلَـٰمًا قَالَ إِنَّا مِنكُمْ وَجِلُونَ (٥٢)
when they entered into his presence and said, ‘Peace!’ He said, ‘We are indeed afraid of you.’ (52)
قَالُوا۟ لَا تَوْجَلْ إِنَّا نُبَشِّرُكَ بِغُلَـٰمٍ عَلِيمٍ (٥٣)

قَالَ أَبَشَّرْتُمُونِى عَلَىٰٓ أَن مَّسَّنِىَ ٱلْكِبَرُ فَبِمَ تُبَشِّرُونَ (٥٤)
He said, ‘Do you give me good news though old age has befallen me? What is the good news that you bring me?’ (54)
قَالُوا۟ بَشَّرْنَـٰكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ فَلَا تَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْقَـٰنِطِينَ (٥٥)
They said, ‘We bring you good news in truth; so do not be despondent.’ (55)
قَالَ وَمَن يَقْنَطُ مِن رَّحْمَةِ رَبِّهِۦٓ إِلَّا ٱلضَّآلُّونَ (٥٦)
He said, ‘Who despairs of his Lord’s mercy except the astray?!’ (56)
قَالَ فَمَا خَطْبُكُمْ أَيُّهَا ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ (٥٧)
He said, ‘O messengers, what is now your errand?’ (57)
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّآ أُرْسِلْنَآ إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍ مُّجْرِمِينَ (٥٨)
They said, ‘We have been sent toward a guilty people, (58)
إِلَّآ ءَالَ لُوطٍ إِنَّا لَمُنَجُّوهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٥٩)
[who shall perish] except the family of Lot. We will indeed deliver all of them, (59)
إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَهُۥ قَدَّرْنَآ ۙ إِنَّهَا لَمِنَ ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (٦٠)
except his wife, [who], We have ordained, will indeed be among those who remain behind.’ (60)

Commentary

Unknown Guests?

From these verses onward, the Quran presents instructive portions from the history of the great Prophets and rebellious nations as clear examples of sincere servants and followers of Satan.

Interestingly, it begins with the story of Abraham’s guests (the same angels who came to him in human form, first bringing glad tidings of the birth of a noble son and then news of the painful punishment of the people of Lot).

This is because in the two preceding verses, the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله was commanded to recount both the station of Allah’s mercy for His servants and His painful punishment. In the story of Abraham’s guests, two living examples of these two attributes are seen, and thus the connection between these and the previous verses becomes clear.

First, He says: "Inform My servants about Abraham’s guests" (وَ نَبِّئْهُمْ عَنْ ضَيْفِ إِبْراهِيمَ‌).

Although "guest" (ḍayf) is singular here, as some great commentators have noted, ḍayf carries both a singular and a plural meaning (guest and guests).

These uninvited guests were the same angels who "when they entered upon Abraham, being unrecognized, first offered him greetings of peace" (إِذْ دَخَلُوا عَلَيْهِ فَقالُوا سَلاماً).

Abraham, as is the duty of a noble and kind host, prepared to entertain them and immediately provided suitable food. However, when the spread was laid out, the unknown guests did not reach for the food. He became frightened by this and did not conceal his fear; he explicitly "said to them: 'Indeed, we are afraid of you'" (قالَ إِنَّا مِنْكُمْ وَجِلُونَ‌) [1]. This fear was due to a custom prevalent at that time, in later times, and even in our era among some peoples, that whenever someone eats another's bread and salt, they will not harm them and feel indebted to them. Therefore, not reaching for the food was taken as a sign of ill intent, malice, or enmity.

But it was not long before the angels relieved Abraham of his anxiety and "said to him: 'Do not be afraid; indeed, we bring you glad tidings of a learned son'" (قالُوا لا تَوْجَلْ إِنَّا نُبَشِّرُكَ بِغُلامٍ عَلِيمٍ‌).

Regarding who is meant by the "learned son" (ghulāmin ‘alīm), in light of other Quranic verses, there is no doubt that it refers to "Isaac" (Isḥāq). For when the angels gave this glad tiding to Abraham, his wife Sarah—who was apparently a barren woman—was present, and this tiding was given to her as well, as we read in Surah Hud, verse 71:

وَ امْرَأَتُهُ قائِمَةٌ فَضَحِكَتْ فَبَشَّرْناها بِإِسْحاقَ‌: "And his wife was standing by and she laughed; so We gave her the glad tidings of Isaac."

We also know that Sarah is the mother of Isaac, and Abraham had previously fathered a son named Ishmael from Hagar (the handmaid he had taken as a wife).

However, Abraham knew well that according to natural standards, the birth of such a child from him was very unlikely. Although nothing is impossible before the power of Allah, considering ordinary and natural standards sparked his wonder. Thus, "he said: 'Do you give me such glad tidings while old age has touched me?!'" (قالَ أَ بَشَّرْتُمُونِي عَلى‌ أَنْ مَسَّنِيَ الْكِبَرُ). "So of what do you give me glad tidings?" (فَبِمَ تُبَشِّرُونَ‌).

Is this glad tiding of yours by the command and decree of Allah, or is it from yourselves? Speak clearly so that I may have greater certainty! The expression "old age has touched me" (massanī al-kibar) indicates that the signs of old age were visible in his white hair and the wrinkles on his face, and he could clearly feel its effects throughout his entire being.

It might be said that Abraham had a prior positive experience in this regard, as his son Ishmael was born during this same old age, and given that, he should not have wondered about the new newborn, Isaac. However, it must be noted that, firstly, according to some commentators, there was a gap of more than ten years between the birth of Ishmael and Isaac, and with the passing of ten years at an advanced age, the probability of fathering a child decreases significantly. Secondly, the occurrence of one instance contrary to normal standards, which might be seen as an exception, does not prevent wonder at similar instances, because in any case, the birth of a child at such an age is a marvelous thing [1].

In any case, the angels did not allow Abraham room for further doubt or wonder. With clarity and decisiveness, "they said: 'We have given you glad tidings in truth'" (قالُوا بَشَّرْناكَ بِالْحَقِ‌). A tiding that was from Allah and by His command, and for that reason, it is the truth and certain.

Following that, for emphasis—supposing that perhaps despair and hopelessness might have overcome Abraham—they said: "Now that it is so, do not be of the despondent" (فَلا تَكُنْ مِنَ الْقانِطِينَ‌).

But Abraham quickly dismissed the idea that despair and hopelessness regarding Allah’s mercy had overcome him; rather, his wonder was solely based on natural standards. Thus, he explicitly "said: 'And who despairs of the mercy of his Lord except the misguided?!'" (قالَ وَ مَنْ يَقْنَطُ مِنْ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّهِ إِلَّا الضَّالُّونَ‌).

The same misguided ones who have not properly recognized Allah and have not realized His infinite power. The God who creates such a magnificent human from a particle of dust, and brings forth a noble child from a humble drop of fluid; the dried date palm bears fruit by His command, and He turns a blazing fire into a garden—who could doubt the power of such a Lord or despair of His mercy?!

In any case, after hearing this glad tiding, Abraham عليه السلام fell into thought that these angels, with those specific conditions, had not come to him solely for the tiding of a son. They must have a more important mission, and this tiding was only a part of it. Therefore, he proceeded to ask: "And he said: 'Then what is your business, O messengers?'" (قالَ فَما خَطْبُكُمْ أَيُّهَا الْمُرْسَلُونَ‌).

"They said: 'Indeed, we have been sent to a criminal people'" (قالُوا إِنَّا أُرْسِلْنا إِلى‌ قَوْمٍ مُجْرِمِينَ‌).

And since they knew that Abraham, with his sense of curiosity—especially regarding such matters—would not be satisfied with this much of an answer, they immediately added that this criminal people is none other than the people of Lot. We are commanded to crush and destroy these shameless, polluted people, "except the family of Lot; indeed, we will save them all" (إِلَّا آلَ لُوطٍ إِنَّا لَمُنَجُّوهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ‌).

However, since the expression "the family of Lot," especially with the emphasis of "all," would include his entire household, even his misguided wife who was aligned with the polytheists—and perhaps Abraham was aware of this—they immediately excluded her and said:

"Except his wife; we have decreed that she shall be among those who remain behind" in the city condemned to destruction and shall not be saved (إِلَّا امْرَأَتَهُ قَدَّرْنا إِنَّها لَمِنَ الْغابِرِينَ‌).

The expression "We have decreed" (qaddarnā) refers to the mission they had from Allah in this regard.

Regarding the meeting of the angels with Abraham, the story of the glad tidings of Isaac’s birth to him, and also the conversation with them regarding the people of Lot, we have discussed these in detail under verses 69 to 76 of Surah Hud (refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 9, page 167 onwards)!

[1]: Although the above verses do not mention Abraham’s hospitality or the guests not touching the food, as we read in the commentary of verses 69 and 70 of Surah Hud, these two points existed (Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 9, pp. 168-169). [1]: Some commentators have said that Abraham was 99 years old at the birth of his first son, Ishmael, and 112 years old at the birth of Isaac!
