Commentary

The Fate of the Sinners of the People of Lot

In the preceding verses, we read the story of the meeting between the angels commissioned to punish the people of Lot and Ibrahim (peace be upon him). In these verses, we read the account of their departure from Ibrahim and their arrival to meet Lot (peace be upon him).

First, it says: "So when the messengers came to the family of Lot" (فَلَمَّا جاءَ آلَ لُوطٍ الْمُرْسَلُونَ ...)

Lot said to them: "Indeed, you are a people unknown [to me]" (قالَ إِنَّكُمْ قَوْمٌ مُنْكَرُونَ). Commentators state that he said this because they appeared to him in the form of handsome and beautiful young men. Knowing how deeply his environment was tainted by the sin of sexual perversion, he worried that the arrival of these guests might cause trouble for him. On one hand, they were guests—honorable and their arrival blessed—but on the other hand, the environment was disgraceful, polluted, and full of difficulties.

Therefore, in the verses of Surah Hud, where this same story is mentioned in another context, the expression "سِي‌ءَ بِهِمْ" is used, meaning this matter was very distressing for Lot, the great Prophet of God, and he was troubled by their arrival. He then said: "This is a distressing day!"

However, the angels did not leave him in suspense for long; they explicitly "said: 'Nay, we have come to you with that which they used to doubt'" (قالُوا بَلْ جِئْناكَ بِما كانُوا فِيهِ يَمْتَرُونَ). This means: We are commissioned with the painful punishment that you repeatedly warned them about, but which they never took seriously.

Then, for emphasis, they said: "And we have come to you with the certain truth" (وَ أَتَيْناكَ بِالْحَقِ), meaning the inevitable torment and the definitive punishment for this faithless and deviant group. Again, for further emphasis, they added: "And indeed, we are truthful" (وَ إِنَّا لَصادِقُونَ).

This meant that this group had burnt all their bridges behind them, and no room remained for intercession or discussion regarding them, so that Lot would not even think of interceding and would know that they absolutely do not deserve it.

Since the small group of believers (Lot’s family, except for his wife) had to escape this peril safely, the angels gave Lot the necessary instructions: "So travel with your family in a portion of the night" (فَأَسْرِ بِأَهْلِكَ بِقِطْعٍ مِنَ اللَّيْلِ), while the eyes of the sinful people are asleep or intoxicated with wine and lust.

But "you follow at their rear" (وَ اتَّبِعْ أَدْبارَهُمْ) so that you may watch over them and ensure no one lags behind.

Furthermore, "let none of you look back" (وَ لا يَلْتَفِتْ مِنْكُمْ أَحَدٌ).

"And go on to where you are commanded" (وَ امْضُوا حَيْثُ تُؤْمَرُونَ), referring to the land of Sham or another location where the people were free from these pollutions.

Then the tone of the discourse changes, and Allah says: "And We revealed to him this decree: that the root of those [sinners] would be cut off by early morning" (وَ قَضَيْنا إِلَيْهِ ذلِكَ الْأَمْرَ أَنَّ دابِرَ هؤُلاءِ مَقْطُوعٌ مُصْبِحِينَ), such that not a single one of them would remain.

(Note) Then the Quran leaves the story here and returns to the beginning, relating a part that was left unsaid there for a reason we will mention later, saying:

"And the people of the city came rejoicing" (وَ جاءَ أَهْلُ الْمَدِينَةِ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ) upon learning of the arrival of Lot's new guests.

In their valley of misguidance and disgrace, they thought they had found delicious prey—handsome and fair-faced youths, and in the house of Lot! The expression "أَهْلُ الْمَدِينَةِ" (the people of the city) indicates that at least a large group of the city's people moved toward Lot's house. This clarifies how brazen, scandalous, and audacious they had become, especially since the phrase "يستبشرون" (congratulating one another) recounts the depth of their pollution. Such a disgraceful act is perhaps rarely seen even among animals and livestock; if someone were to do it, they would at least do so with concealment, secrecy, and a sense of shame. But this wicked and base nation was openly congratulating one another!!

Lot, hearing their clamor, fell into a strange terror and feared for his guests, for he did not yet know that these guests were agents of punishment and angels of the All-Powerful, All-Subduing God. Thus, he stood before them and "said: 'Indeed, these are my guests, so do not disgrace me'" (قالَ إِنَّ هؤُلاءِ ضَيْفِي فَلا فَضَحُونِ). That is to say: even if we set aside the matters of God, the Prophet, and reward and punishment, at the very least, this human and traditional principle—which exists among all human beings, whether believer or disbeliever—dictates that a guest must be respected. What kind of humans are you that you do not understand even the simplest human principles? If you have no religion, at least be free-spirited!

Then he added: Come and "fear Allah and do not shame me" (وَ اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ لا تُخْزُونِ) before my guests. [1]

But they were so audacious and, so to speak, thick-skinned that not only did they feel no shame, but they even demanded something from the Prophet Lot. As if he had committed a crime, they began to protest and "said: 'Did we not forbid you from [protecting] any of the worlds?'" (قالُوا أَ وَ لَمْ نَنْهَكَ عَنِ الْعالَمِينَ). Why did you act contrary to our word and not follow what we said?! This was because that tribe and population were stingy and miserly people who never hosted anyone in their homes. Incidentally, their cities were on the route of caravans, and it is said that to prevent anyone from stopping there, they would perform this heinous act with some of the newcomers. Gradually, it became a habit for them. Thus, whenever the Prophet Lot learned that a stranger had entered that land, he would invite him to his home so he would not fall into their clutches. But when they became aware of this, they grew angry and explicitly told him he had no right to admit guests into his house thereafter!

[1]: In the above verses, Lot asks this shameless people sometimes not to bring him to "fadihah" (disgrace) and sometimes to "khizy" (shame). Fadihah in its original linguistic sense means the uncovering of something, and subsequently, it came to mean the appearance of a defect; its equivalent is "scandalizing" (it is as if Lot wants to make them understand that this action of theirs ruins his reputation before these guests, and they will realize how polluted the people of my city are). However, khizy originally means distancing, and subsequently, it came to mean shame (it is as if Lot wants to say: do not make me ashamed before these guests and do not distance me from them). Therefore, it seems that the word "alameen" (the worlds) in the above verse refers to wayfarers and individuals who were not residents of that city and land but were passing through.

In any case, when Lot saw this audacity and impudence, he approached them from another angle, perhaps to wake them from the sleep of negligence and the intoxication of deviation and disgrace. Turning to them, he said: Why do you follow a deviant path? If your intention is to satisfy the sexual instinct, why do not you enter through the legitimate way and sound marriage? "These are my daughters (I am ready to give them to you in marriage) if you must act" (قالَ هؤُلاءِ بَناتِي إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فاعِلِينَ). Undoubtedly, Lot's daughters were limited in number, while that population was large, but Lot's goal was to complete the proof against them and say: I am ready to make even this sacrifice to preserve the dignity of my guests and save you from the quagmire of corruption. Some have also said that "these are my daughters" refers to the daughters of the city, whom he called his daughters in his capacity as a spiritual and moral father (but the first interpretation is closer to the meaning of the verse). It goes without saying that Lot did not want to marry his daughters to misguided polytheists; rather, his goal was: come and believe, and then I will marry my daughters to you.

But alas for the intoxication of lust, the intoxication of deviation, and the intoxication of pride and stubbornness! If a shred of human morality and human emotion existed in them, it would have been enough to make them ashamed before such logic, or at least to make them turn back from Lot's house and feel bashful. But not only were they not humbled, they increased their audacity and sought to reach out toward Lot's guests!! It is here that God addresses the Prophet of Islam, saying: "By your life, [O Prophet], indeed they were wandering blindly in their intoxication!" (لَعَمْرُكَ إِنَّهُمْ لَفِي سَكْرَتِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ). In Surah Hud, following a similar discussion, we read that the angels revealed their true nature, turned to Lot, and said: Do not fear, they will not harm you. And in verse 37 of Surah Qamar, we read: When they increased their audacity and decided to assault the guests, We blinded their eyes (وَ لَقَدْ راوَدُوهُ عَنْ ضَيْفِهِ فَطَمَسْنا أَعْيُنَهُمْ). As mentioned in some narrations, one of the angels threw a handful of dust at their faces, and they all became blind (and returned crying out).

Here, the Divine word regarding this people reaches its climax, and in two concise and short verses, it describes their grim fate in a decisive, crushing, and very exemplary manner, saying: "So the [awful] cry seized them at sunrise" (فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الصَّيْحَةُ مُشْرِقِينَ). This "cry" (saihah) might have been the sound of a great thunderbolt or the sound of a terrifying earthquake. In any case, it was a shout from the terror of which they all lost consciousness or lost their lives. We know that when sound waves exceed a certain limit, they become painful and terrifying, and beyond that, they render a person unconscious or completely shut down vital organs, and may even destroy buildings.

But We did not stop at this; rather, We completely overturned their city: "We turned it upside down" (فَجَعَلْنا عالِيَها سافِلَها). Even this punishment was not enough for them: "and We rained upon them stones of baked clay" (وَ أَمْطَرْنا عَلَيْهِمْ حِجارَةً مِنْ سِجِّيلٍ). This rain of stones might have been aimed at those who were not destroyed by that terrifying cry or had not gone under the rubble, and it might also have been to obliterate the vile corpses and the traces of this people, such that after this rain of stones, if someone passed by that land, they could not easily believe that there were once prosperous cities in this region! The descent of these three punishments (the terrifying cry, the overturning, and the rain of stones) was each individually sufficient to destroy a nation, but because of the gravity of their sin and their audacity in surrendering to pollution and disgrace, and also as a lesson for others, Allah multiplied their punishment.

It is here that the Quran draws an educational and moral conclusion, saying: "Indeed in that are signs for those who discern" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِلْمُتَوَسِّمِينَ)—those who, with their special sagacity, alertness, and insight, discover a process from every sign, a truth from every hint, and an important and instructive point from every detail. [1]

[1]: Mutawassim is from the root wasm (rhyming with rasm), meaning to leave a mark. A "mutawassim" is someone who, from the slightest trace, perceives realities; its equivalents in Persian are alert, sagacious, and intelligent.

But do not imagine that their traces have completely vanished; no, "And indeed, they [the ruins] are on an established road" (وَ إِنَّها لَبِسَبِيلٍ مُقِيمٍ). If you do not believe, arise and go and look at the ruins of these afflicted cities which lie on the path of travelers on the way to Sham (from the direction of Medina), and reflect; reflect and take heed; take heed and return to Allah, and take the path of repentance, and wash the pollutions from your heart and soul.

Again, as further emphasis and an invitation for the believers to reflect and ponder on this exemplary story, it adds: "Indeed in that is a sign for the believers" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ). How is it possible for a person to have faith and read this shaking account and not take lessons?!

Regarding the meaning of "Sijjil" and why a rain of stones fell upon this polluted people, and why their city was overturned, and why the moment the punishment descended was in the morning, and why Lot's family was commanded not to look back, as well as regarding the prohibition of homosexuality in heavenly religions and its philosophy, and also the morality of the people of Lot, we discussed sufficiently under the verses of Surah Hud (refer to Volume 9 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 189 to 198). However, some points remain which are presented below.

Key Points

1- What is meant by "بِقِطْعٍ مِنَ اللَّيْلِ"?

"Qit'" means the darkness of the night. The late Tabarsi in Maimau' al-Bayan says: It seems "qit'" is the plural of "qit'ah" (piece), and for this reason, he understood from this expression in the above verse the passing of the major part of the night. However, from what Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, it appears that "qit'" means "piece" and is singular. Nevertheless, many commentators have taken this word to mean the end of the night and the time of dawn (Sahar). Perhaps this interpretation is due to some other verses of the Quran which explicitly say regarding the family of Lot: "We saved them at dawn" (نَجَّيْناهُمْ بِسَحَرٍ - Surah Al-Qamar, verse 34). That is, they departed at the very time when the polluted lust-worshippers were submerged in the sleep of negligence, and the intoxication of wine, pride, and lust were mingled in their being, and the city was completely ready for the departure of Lot's family. And it is remarkable that the start of their crushing punishment was also in the morning, at the time of sunrise. Perhaps this time was chosen because when the attacking group became blind and returned to their homes, they reflected a little; therefore, He gave them a respite that night so they might repent and return, and rise to compensate. It is also understood from some narrations that some of them, when they returned to their homes, swore that in the morning they would not leave even one person from Lot's family alive. But before they could take a step in this direction, the Divine punishment mowed them down! [1]

2- Commentary on the phrase "وَ امْضُوا حَيْثُ تُؤْمَرُونَ".

We said the angels advised Lot's family to move at the end of the night toward the very point they were commanded. However, no further explanation regarding this point is seen in the verses of the Quran. For this reason, commentators have various statements: some have said they were commanded to go toward the land of Sham, the environment of which was relatively pure. Others have said that the angels specified a certain village and advised them to go there. In Tafsir al-Mizan, it is only inferred from this phrase that they had a kind of Divine guidance and a real guide with them on their path and acted according to it.

3- The Relationship between "Mutawassim" and "Mu'min"

In the above verses, we saw that it sometimes says "in the exemplary story of the people of Lot, there are signs for the mutawassimin" and sometimes says "for the mu'minin." The combination of these two expressions makes us understand that true believers are mutawassim, meaning they are sagacious, quick-witted, and completely alert. In a narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read that he was asked about the interpretation of "إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِلْمُتَوَسِّمِينَ." He said: "It refers to the Muslim Ummah." Then he added: "The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said: Beware of the sagacity (firasah) of the believer, for he looks with the light of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic!" [1] In another narration, we read that Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام said: "The mutawassimin are the Imams." [2] And it is narrated from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, who said: "The mutawassim was the Prophet, and I after him, and then the Imams from my progeny." [3]

4- The Intoxication of Lust and Pride!

Although the intoxication of wine is famous, there are intoxications worse than that of wine, including the intoxication of status and the intoxication of lust. In the above verses, we read that God swears by the life of His Prophet that this group was wandering and bewildered in their intoxication, such that they could not see the clearest road to salvation. It reached a point where a great Prophet like Lot was willing to marry his daughters to them so they might be satisfied through the halal and legitimate way and be saved from sin, pollution, and disgrace, but they still rejected him! Furthermore, this great Prophet gives us this instructive lesson: to fight against corruption, do not rely only on negation, but one must also rely on affirmation. That is, human instincts must be satisfied through the correct way so they do not lean toward corruption. Although the people of Lot were exceptional corrupt individuals upon whom this program was not effective, usually this method is one of the most effective methods. When we want to fight against unhealthy entertainment, we must primarily strive to provide healthy entertainment for the people; the same applies to other programs. Interestingly, we read in some narrations that Lot, this highly steadfast Prophet, was engaged in preaching among this base and low population for about thirty years, but no one believed in him except his family (with the exception of his wife). [1] How magnificent is all this steadfastness, especially among such low-lifes that a person becomes weary from even one hour of living among them, and how painful it is to endure such a wife! In Surah Adh-Dhariyat, verses 35 and 36, we read: "So We brought out whoever was in it of the believers. But We did not find within it other than one house of Muslims." And from here it also becomes clear that Divine punishment never burns the dry and the wet together; even if there is one true and duty-conscious believer, He saves him.

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 385. [1], [2], [3]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 23. [1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 382.

Ayahs

وَإِن كَانَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْأَيْكَةِ لَظَـٰلِمِينَ (٧٨)
Indeed the inhabitants of Aykah were [also] wrongdoers. (78)

وَكَانُوا۟ يَنْحِتُونَ مِنَ ٱلْجِبَالِ بُيُوتًا ءَامِنِينَ (٨٢)

Commentary

The End of Two Oppressive Nations

In these verses, the Quran refers to two other segments of the history of past nations under the titles of "As-hab al-Aykah" (the Dwellers of the Wood) and "As-hab al-Hijr" (the Dwellers of the Stone-land), completing the cautionary discussions regarding the people of Lut (Lot) mentioned in the preceding verses.

First, It says: "The Dwellers of the Wood were surely wrongdoers and oppressors" (وَ إِنْ كانَ أَصْحابُ الْأَيْكَةِ لَظالِمِينَ) [^1]

"So We took vengeance on them," and punished them for their oppressions, transgressions, and rebellions (فَانْتَقَمْنا مِنْهُمْ). And the lands of this group and the people of Lut, whose story has passed, are situated on your open highway and are clearly visible (وَ إِنَّهُما لَبِإِمامٍ مُبِينٍ).

Open your eyes and look at their fate, then take a lesson.

Who are the As-hab al-Aykah?

Many commentators and lexicographers have stated that "Aykah" means tangled trees or a thicket, and "As-hab al-Aykah" are the people of Shu'ayb who lived in a land abundant with water and trees between Hijaz and Sham (Syria).

They enjoyed a luxurious life and great wealth, and for this reason, pride and negligence had overtaken them; specifically, they engaged in giving short measure (cheating in trade) and spreading corruption on earth.

Shu'ayb, that great Prophet, warned them against these actions and invited them to monotheism

[^1]: The word "in" (إِنْ) in this verse is not the conditional "in," but rather "in mukhaffafah min al-muthaqqalah" (the lightened form of the emphasized 'inna'), and the underlying meaning is: "Indeed, the Dwellers of the Wood were wrongdoers."

and the path of truth. However, as we saw in the verses of Surah Hud, they did not submit to the truth and were ultimately destroyed by a painful punishment. Intense heat gripped them for several consecutive days, and on the final day, a large cloud appeared in the sky. They sought refuge under the shadow of the cloud, but a thunderbolt descended and destroyed those tyrants.

Perhaps the Quran's emphasis on the term "As-hab al-Aykah" (the Dwellers of the Wooded Lands) is because It wishes to say that despite all the blessings He had bestowed upon them, they showed ingratitude instead of thankfulness, established oppression and tyranny, and thus the thunderbolt wiped them and their trees away.

A more detailed account of their circumstances—explicitly naming Shu'ayb—is found in Surah Ash-Shu'ara, from verse 176 to 190.

Additionally, it should be noted that the phrase "So We took vengeance on them" (فَانْتَقَمْنا مِنْهُمْ) may refer to both the people of Lut and the Dwellers of the Wood, because immediately after this phrase we read: "And indeed, both of them are on an open highway" (وَ إِنَّهُما لَبِإِمامٍ مُبِينٍ).

In the commentary of the phrase "Indeed, both of them are on an open highway" (إِنَّهُما لَبِإِمامٍ مُبِينٍ), the well-known and famous view is that it refers to the city of the people of Lut and the city of the Dwellers of the Wood, and the word "Imam" means a path or road (as it is derived from the root "amm" meaning to intend or head towards, since a person traverses roads to reach a destination).

Some have also suggested the possibility that "Imam Mubin" refers to the Preserved Tablet (al-Lawh al-Mahfuz), drawing an analogy from verse 12 of Surah Ya-Sin. However, this possibility is very remote, because the Quran intends to present a lesson for the people, and the mere presence of the names of these two cities

in the Preserved Tablet cannot have an impact on people taking a lesson. In contrast, the presence of these two cities on the route of caravans and other passersby could leave a deep impression on them—that they might pause there for a moment, reflect, and let their lesson-seeking hearts observe through their eyes, considering this disaster-stricken land a mirror of warning. Sometimes by the land of the people of Lut and sometimes by the land of the Dwellers of the Wood, they might finally let a flood of tears flow from their eyes over their fate!

As for the "As-hab al-Hijr" (the Dwellers of the Stone-land), that rebellious people who lived a luxurious life in a land called "Hijr" and to whom their great Prophet "Salih" was sent for guidance, It says: "The Dwellers of the Stone-land denied the messengers" (وَ لَقَدْ كَذَّبَ أَصْحابُ الْحِجْرِ الْمُرْسَلِينَ)! Regarding where this city was located, some commentators and historians have written that it was a city on the caravan route between Medina and Sham, at a distance of one stage from "Wadi al-Qura" and south of "Tayma," and today almost no trace of it remains.

It is said that in the past, this city was one of the commercial hubs of Arabia, so significant that Ptolemy mentioned it as a trading city in his writings, and Pliny, the Roman geographer, refers to it as "Hegra."

In a narration, we read that in the ninth year of the Hijrah, when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله led an army to Tabuk to repel the Roman forces, the soldiers of Islam wanted to stop at this station. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله prevented them and said: "This is the very region of the people of Thamud upon whom divine punishment

descended." [^1] It is also noteworthy that the Quran, regarding the Dwellers of the Stone-land (and similarly regarding the people of Nuh, the people of Shu'ayb, and the people of Lut in the verses of Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verses 105, 123, and 160 respectively, and some other past nations), says they denied "the messengers," while the apparent matter shows that each had no more than one Prophet and they denied only him.

This expression is perhaps because the program and goal of the Prophets are so interconnected that denying one of them is equivalent to denying all of them.

The possibility has also been raised that these nations had multiple Prophets, among whom one was more prominent and well-known, but the first commentary seems more correct.

In any case, the Quran continues regarding the "Dwellers of the Stone-land": "We gave them Our signs, but they turned away from them" (وَ آتَيْناهُمْ آياتِنا فَكانُوا عَنْها مُعْرِضِينَ). The expression "turning away" (i'rad) shows that they were not even willing to hear these signs or cast a glance at them.

On the contrary, in the affairs of their worldly life, they were so industrious that: "They used to hew out houses from the mountains, [feeling] secure" (وَ كانُوا يَنْحِتُونَ مِنَ الْجِبالِ بُيُوتاً آمِنِينَ). This indicates, firstly, that their region was mountainous, and secondly,

[^1]: A'lam al-Quran, Khuza'ili, page 292.

that they possessed an advanced material civilization that enabled them to prepare secure homes inside the mountains that were completely resistant to storms, floods, and even earthquakes.

It is strange that man performs such solid construction for a few days of worldly life, yet is so negligent regarding his eternal and everlasting life that he is sometimes not even willing to hear the word of Allah or look at His signs!

Well, what can be expected for such a people, except that according to the law of "Divine Selection of the Fittest" and not granting the right to continue life to nations that become entirely corrupt and corrupting, a destroying calamity should descend upon them and annihilate them. Therefore, the Quran says: "But the [awful] cry seized them in the morning" (فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الصَّيْحَةُ مُصْبِحِينَ). This cry was the sound of a deadly thunderbolt that descended upon their homes, and it was so crushing, jarring, and terrifying that it cast their lifeless bodies upon the ground.

Evidence for this is verse 13 of Surah Fussilat: "But if they turn away, then say, 'I have warned you of a thunderbolt like the thunderbolt of 'Aad and Thamud.'"

Neither those sky-reaching mountains, nor those secure homes, nor the powerful physiques of this rebellious people, nor that abundant wealth—none could resist this divine punishment. Thus, at the end of their story, He says: "So all that they used to earn did not avail them" (فَما أَغْنى‌ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ).

In Surah Ash-Shu'ara, within verses 141 to 158, their circumstances are described in more detail, which, Allah willing, will be discussed in the commentary of those verses.

Commentary

The Dividers and the Eclectics!

Since man’s perpetual struggle stems from the lack of a correct ideology and belief—in short, a lack of commitment to the Origin (Allah) and the Resurrection—after describing the states of peoples such as the folk of Lut, Shu’ayb, and Salih, who were afflicted by those calamities, the text returns to the issues of "Monotheism" (Tawhid) and "Resurrection" (Ma'ad), referring to both in a single verse: "We created not the heavens and the earth and all that is between them save with truth" (وَ ما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِ‌).

Both the system governing them is truth and calculated, and the purpose of their creation is truth. For this reason, this wondrous order and precise, organized creation are clear evidence of a Wise and Powerful Creator who is Himself Truth; rather, He is the very Essence of Truth. Every truth is true only insofar as it harmonizes with His infinite existence, and whatever is other than Him and has no link to Him is falsehood and vanity.

This pertains to Monotheism. Then, regarding the Resurrection, He says: "And surely the Hour is coming" (وَ إِنَّ السَّاعَةَ لَآتِيَةٌ).

And if it arrives late, it shall surely arrive in the end.

It is not unlikely that the first sentence serves as a proof for the second, for the truthfulness of this vast universe is realized only if it were not created merely for these few days of life filled with distress, but rather for a higher purpose that justifies this great creation. Therefore, the truthfulness of the creation of the heavens, the earth, and the universe is itself a proof that we have a Resurrection ahead of us; otherwise, creation would be in vain. (Reflect deeply).

Following this, He commands His Prophet to show gentleness and love in the face of their stubbornness, ignorance, prejudice, sabotage, and fierce opposition: "So overlook [their faults] with gracious forgiveness—a forgiveness that is beautiful and not even accompanied by reproach" (فَاصْفَحِ الصَّفْحَ الْجَمِيلَ‌).

For you, possessing clear proof in the path of the call and the mission you are charged with—to strengthen the foundations of the Origin and Resurrection in the hearts of the people—have no need for violence. Logic and reason are with you. Furthermore, violence against the ignorant often leads to an increase in their own violence and prejudice.

"Safh" (صفح) means the surface of anything, such as the surface (page) of the face ^1. For this reason, "Fasfah" (فاصفح) has come to mean "turn away" and "overlook." And since

^1: Firuzabadi says in Al-Qamus: The slope of a mountain is called "Safh," the flat side of a sword is "Safh," and the face is also "Safh." "Safh" means the side or surface of anything.

turning away from something is sometimes due to indifference, anger, or the like, and sometimes due to noble pardon and forgiveness, the verse immediately describes it with the word "Jamil" (beautiful) to convey the second meaning.

In a narration from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, we read regarding the interpretation of this verse: "It means forgiveness without rebuke or reproach." ^1 A similar hadith is narrated from Imam Zayn al-Abidin عليه السلام as well. [^2]

The next verse—as a group of commentators have stated—is actually like a proof for the necessity of pardon, forgiveness, and "gracious overlooking." It says: "Surely your Lord is the All-Creator, the All-Knowing" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ الْخَلَّاقُ الْعَلِيمُ‌). He knows that all people are not the same; He is aware of the inner secrets, temperaments, levels of intellectual growth, and various emotions. You should not expect all of them to be identical; rather, you must deal with them with a spirit of pardon and forgiveness so that they may be gradually nurtured and come to the path of truth.

Of course, this does not mean that people are compelled in their ways and the actions they perform; rather, it is merely an indication of a pedagogical instruction related to the differences in thinking and aptitudes.

It is also necessary to mention that some have imagined this command was specific to the period of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله life in Mecca, and that after he migrated to Medina and the Muslims gained power, this command was abrogated and replaced by the command for Jihad.

However, considering that this command appears in Medinan Surahs as well (such as Surah al-Baqarah, Surah al-Nur, Surah al-Taghabun, and Surah al-Ma'idah), where in some the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is commanded to overlook and forgive, and in others the believers are, it becomes clear that this is a general and eternal instruction. Incidentally, it does not contradict the command for Jihad at all, as each has its own specific place. In some instances, progress must be made through pardon and forgiveness, but when pardon and forgiveness lead to audacity, insolence, and exploitation by the other party, there is no choice but to take firm action.

Then He comforts His Prophet, telling him never to be worried by the violence of the enemies, their large numbers, or the abundant material resources at their disposal, for Allah has placed at his disposal blessings that nothing can equal. He says: "And We have certainly given you the seven of the oft-repeated [verses] and the Great Quran" (وَ لَقَدْ آتَيْناكَ سَبْعاً مِنَ الْمَثانِي وَ الْقُرْآنَ الْعَظِيمَ‌). We know that "Sab'a" (سبع) linguistically means "seven" and "Mathani" (مثانی) means "doubles" or "oft-repeated." Most commentators and narrations have taken "the seven of the oft-repeated" as an allusion to Surah al-Hamd (al-Fatiha), because Surah al-Hamd, according to the well-known view, consists of seven verses. It is called "Mathani" (doubles) because, due to its importance and the greatness of its content, it was revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله twice, or because it consists of two parts (half is praise of Allah and half is the requests of the servants), or because it is recited twice in every prayer. ^1 [^2] Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that "seven" refers to the seven

^1: In a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that Allah says: "I have divided the prayer (Surah al-Hamd) between Myself and My servant into two parts: half belongs to Me and the other half to My servants" (Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1, p. 17). [^2]: Refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, pp. 28-29.

great Surahs at the beginning of the Quran, and "Mathani" is an allusion to the Quran itself, because the Quran was revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله twice: once as a whole, and once gradually according to needs and at different times. Therefore, "the seven of the oft-repeated" would mean seven important Surahs from the collection of the Quran.

Additionally, they have taken verse 23 of Surah al-Zumar as evidence for this meaning: "Allah has sent down the best statement: a consistent Book, oft-repeated (Mathani)..." (اللَّهُ نَزَّلَ أَحْسَنَ الْحَدِيثِ كِتاباً مُتَشابِهاً مَثانِيَ‌).

However, the first interpretation seems more correct, especially since it has been repeatedly interpreted as Surah al-Hamd in narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام.

Raghib in Al-Mufradat considers the application of the word "Mathani" to the Quran to be because its verses are recited repeatedly, and this very renewal and repetition protect it from the ravages of time (furthermore, the reality of the Quran has a new repetition and manifestation in every age, all of which necessitates it being called Mathani). In any case, mentioning the "Great Quran" after mentioning Surah al-Hamd—even though it is part of the Quran—is evidence of the importance and greatness of this Surah. It is common to mention a part of a whole independently alongside the whole due to its significance, and this expression is abundant in Arabic and Persian literature.

In short, Allah conveys this reality to His Prophet: you possess such a vast treasure—a treasure like the Quran, as great as the entire universe; a treasure that is entirely light and blessing, a lesson and a program, a guide and a path-opener. Especially Surah al-Hamd, whose content is so sublime that in a brief

moment, it connects man to Allah and compels his soul to bow, submit, and supplicate at His threshold.

Following the mention of this great blessing, He gives four important commands to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. First, He says: "Never strain your eyes toward the material blessings We have given to groups of the disbelievers" (لا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلى‌ ما مَتَّعْنا بِهِ أَزْواجاً مِنْهُمْ‌) ^1. These material blessings are neither lasting nor free from trouble; even in their best state, maintaining them is extremely difficult. Therefore, they are not something that should attract your eye or be considered significant compared to that great spiritual blessing (the Quran) that Allah has given you. Then He adds: "And do not grieve over them [because of their wealth and material blessings]" (وَ لا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلى‌ ما مَتَّعْنا بِهِ أَزْواجاً مِنْهُمْ‌). In fact, the first command is about not fixing one's eyes on material blessings, and the second command is about not grieving over being deprived of them.

The possibility has also been suggested in the interpretation of the phrase "and do not grieve over them" that it means: if they do not believe in you, do not be grieved, for they have no value or merit. However, the first interpretation seems more consistent with the preceding phrases.

In any case, a similar theme appears more clearly in Surah Ta-Ha, verse 131: "And strain not your eyes toward that which We have given for enjoyment to [various] groups of them, [its being but] the splendor of the worldly life that We may test them thereby. And the provision of your Lord is better and more enduring" (وَ لا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلى‌ ما مَتَّعْنا بِهِ أَزْواجاً مِنْهُمْ زَهْرَةَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا لِنَفْتِنَهُمْ فِيهِ وَ رِزْقُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ وَ أَبْقى‌). These are the flowers of the life of this world (unstable flowers that

soon wither and shed their petals). This is because We wish to test them with it; what Allah has provided for you is better and more lasting.

The third command He gives to the Prophet is regarding humility, modesty, and gentleness toward the believers. He says: "And lower your wing for the believers" (وَ اخْفِضْ جَناحَكَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

This expression is a beautiful metaphor for humility, love, and kindness, just as birds, when they wish to show affection to their chicks, take them under their wings and manifest the most stirring scene of emotion, protecting them from accidents and enemies and keeping them from scattering! In fact, this short metaphorical expression hides a wealth of meaning within it!

Additionally, mentioning this sentence after the previous commands might be an indication: do not be humble or modest before the affluent disbelievers because of their material blessings; rather, direct your humility, modesty, love, and emotions toward the believers, even if their hands are empty of worldly wealth.

Finally, He gives the fourth command to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: stand firm against these faithless and wealthy individuals "and say: Surely I am the plain warner" (وَ قُلْ إِنِّي أَنَا النَّذِيرُ الْمُبِينُ‌).

Say: I warn you that Allah has declared He will send down a punishment upon you "just as We sent down upon the dividers" (كَما أَنْزَلْنا عَلَى الْمُقْتَسِمِينَ‌).

"Those dividers who fragmented the Quran [into parts]" (الَّذِينَ جَعَلُوا الْقُرْآنَ عِضِينَ‌) ^1. They took what was to their benefit and set aside what was to their detriment. In reality, instead of the Divine Book and its commands being their leader and guide, they turned it into a tool and made it a means to reach their sinister goals. If a single word was in their favor, they clung to it, and if thousands of words were to their detriment, they cast them aside!

Key Points

1- The Quran: A Great Divine Blessing

In the above verses, Allah announces to His Prophet—as a warning to all Muslims of the world—that this great Heavenly Book is a vast and grand treasure, a peerless blessing placed at the disposal of you Muslims. It is an eternal program that, if implemented in human life, will build a world that is prosperous, free, safe, secure, and filled with spirituality.

This is a reality that even others acknowledge, believing that if Muslims had revitalized this Quran and its teachings and submitted to its commands, they would have been so powerful and advanced that no one could have imposed their hegemony over them.

This Surah al-Hamd (the seven of the oft-repeated), which is called the "Opening of the Book" (Fatihat al-Kitab), the starter, and the index of the Quran, is a complete course in the lesson of life:

^1: "Idhin" (عضین) is the plural of "Idhah" (عضه), meaning to scatter or divide, and it is also said of every part of a thing. Therefore, "Idhin" means parts and sections.

Attention to the Great Origin who nurtures all the worlds on the path of evolutionary perfection, whose specific and general mercy encompasses all; attention to the Great Court, belief in which places the most precise control over human actions.

Non-reliance on other than Allah, and non-submission or humility before other than Him, and finally, stepping onto the Straight Path in which there is no deviation—it leans neither to the East nor to the West, neither to excess nor to deficiency; there is no misguidance in it, nor the wrath and anger of the Lord.

These constitute a collection that, if implemented in the human soul, is sufficient to build a sublime and perfected personality.

But alas, this great treasure has fallen into the hands of those who have neither reached its depths nor its high value. Sometimes, ignorant people are found among them who abandon its verses and stretch out their hands in need toward the laws and programs made by humans who are themselves captives in the claws of lusts and are, at the very least, full of intellectual inadequacies. Or they sell its teachings for a "miserable price" and a negligible value, or the slight progress of the material civilization of others attracts their attention so much that they become heedless of what they themselves possess.

The meaning of this statement is not that we should ignore material progress; rather, the goal is that we should not limit and confine our everything to it. Incidentally, the Quran is not only a rich and abundant source of spirituality but is also considered an effective program for material progress and welfare, as we have explained in relevant verses in the past and will do so in the future, Allah willing.

2- Straining One's Eyes Toward the Resources of Others is a Cause of Decline

There are many narrow-minded individuals who are always watching this person and that person to see what they have.

They constantly compare their material situation with others and suffer from material deficiencies in this comparison, even if those others obtained these resources at the cost of losing human values and independence of personality.

This way of thinking, which is a sign of insufficient growth, an internal sense of inferiority, and a lack of ambition, will be one of the effective factors of backwardness in life—even in material life.

One who feels a sense of personality within himself, instead of being trapped in such an ugly and painful comparison, employs his intellectual and physical strength in the path of his own growth and progress. He says to himself: "I am not less than others, and there is no reason why I cannot progress more than them. Why should I fix my eyes on their wealth and status? I will produce better and more myself."

In fact, material life is not his goal or ambition; he wants it, but only insofar as it helps his spirituality. He pursues it, but only insofar as it preserves his independence and freedom. He neither runs after it greedily nor exchanges his everything for it—for this is not the exchange of free men and servants of Allah—nor does he do anything that makes him dependent on others.

In a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Whoever casts his gaze at what is in the hands of others, his grief will increase and his anger will not be healed!" ^1

^1: Tafsir al-Safi, under the verses being discussed.

3- The Humility of the Leader

In the verses of the Quran, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is repeatedly advised to be humble, kind, soft, and gentle toward the believers. This is not exclusive to the Prophet of Islam; anyone who carries the duty of leadership within a wide or limited scope must apply this principle, which is one of the fundamental principles of correct management. For the great treasure of a leader is the love and spiritual bond of his followers with him, and this cannot be achieved without humility, cheerfulness, and goodwill. The violence and cruelty of leaders are always major factors in the division and scattering of people from around them.

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, in his letter to Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr, says: "Lower your wing for them! Be soft-sided toward them, keep an open face for them, and treat them equally even in your glances and looks." ^1

4- Who are the "Dividers" (Muqtasimin)?

Divine programs undoubtedly generally protect the interests of all humans, but in appearance and at first glance, some usually align with our desires and some go against our desires. It is here that true believers are distinguished from false claimants. The first group accepts everything in its entirety, even where it is apparently not to their benefit, and they say: "All is from our Lord" (كُلٌّ مِنْ عِنْدِ رَبِّنا), and they do not believe in any kind of fragmentation, division, or discrimination among Divine rulings.

But those who have diseased hearts and even want to take the religion and the ruling of Allah

^1: Nahj al-Balagha, Section of Letters, Letter 27.

into the service of their own interests, accept only that part which is to their benefit and cast the rest behind them. They fragment the verses of the Quran, and sometimes even a single verse; they accept the part they see in the path of their own inclination and set the other part aside.

It is no honor to raise the tune of "We believe in some and disbelieve in some" (نُؤْمِنُ بِبَعْضٍ وَ نَكْفُرُ بِبَعْضٍ‌) like some past nations, for all world-worshippers do the same. What is the criterion for distinguishing the followers of truth from the supporters of falsehood is that very submission in the face of that part of the commands that does not harmonize with our inclinations, whims, and apparent interests. It is here that the pure is distinguished from the impure, and the believer from the hypocrite.

In addition to what we said above, other interpretations have also been mentioned for "Muqtasimin." Even Qurtubi in his commentary mentioned seven interpretations for this word, many of which seem inappropriate, but we mention some that are not irrelevant below.

Among them is that a group of the leaders of the polytheists used to stand at the roads and alleys of Mecca during the Hajj season, and each of them would say something to the newcomers about the Prophet and the Quran to make them pessimistic. Some would say he is mad and what he says is incoherent; some would say he is a sorcerer and his Quran is part of his sorcery; some called him a poet and falsely considered the soul-stirring melody of these heavenly verses to be poetry; some introduced the Prophet as a soothsayer (Kahin) and the unseen news of the Quran as a type of soothsaying. For this reason, they were called "Muqtasimin" (dividers) because they had divided the roads and passages of Mecca among themselves with a calculated plan.

And there is no obstacle to both this interpretation and the interpretation we mentioned being combined within the concept of the verse.

Commentary

Proclaim Your Creed Openly!

In these verses, which are the final verses of Surah "al-Hijr," the discourse first addresses the fate of the "partitioners" (those who divide) mentioned in the preceding verses, stating: "So, by your Lord, We will surely question them all" (فَوَ رَبِّكَ لَنَسْئَلَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ...)

"About all that they used to do" (عَمَّا كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ‌). It is clear that Allah’s questioning is not for the purpose of discovering hidden or covered matters, for He is aware of the secrets of the inner and the outer, and not an atom in the heavens or the earth is hidden from His infinite knowledge. Therefore, the aforementioned questioning is for the purpose of making the person understand themselves so they may realize the ugliness of their deeds, or it serves as a form of psychological punishment. This is because an interrogation regarding wrongful acts, especially when accompanied by reproach and blame—and that too in a world where man becomes closer to and more aware of realities—is painful and agonizing. Thus, in reality, these questions are part of their punishment.

Furthermore, the generality of "about all that they used to do" (عَمَّا كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ‌) indicates that every human action, without exception, will be questioned. This itself is a lesson for all human beings not to remain heedless of their actions for even a moment. The view of some commentators who limited this questioning to the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and faith in the Prophets, or related it only to the idols of the polytheists, is a claim without evidence; rather, the concept of the verse is entirely universal.

As for the question of how Allah emphasizes the issue of questioning in the above verse, while in Surah ar-Rahman, verse 39, we read: "On that Day, neither man nor jinn will be asked about his sin" (فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لا يُسْئَلُ عَنْ ذَنْبِهِ إِنْسٌ وَ لا جَانٌ‌), the answer has been provided previously. In summary: there are stages in the Resurrection; in some stages, people are questioned, and in other stages, there is no questioning or answering as matters are self-evident, or there is no verbal questioning because, according to verse 65 of Surah Ya-Sin, seals are placed upon the mouths, and only the limbs and even the skin are questioned [^1].

Then, He gives a decisive command to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: In the face of the clamor of the polytheists and criminals, do not allow weakness, fear, or lethargy to overcome you, nor remain silent; rather, "Proclaim openly what you are commanded" and reveal the truths of religion with clarity (فَاصْدَعْ بِما تُؤْمَرُ). "And turn away from the polytheists" and be indifferent toward them (وَ أَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْمُشْرِكِينَ‌).

"Fasda’" (فاصدع) is derived from the root "sad’" (صدع), which linguistically means splitting in general, or splitting hard objects. Since by splitting something its interior is revealed, this word has come to mean manifesting, disclosing, and making apparent. A severe headache is also called "suda’" (صداع) because it feels as if it wants to split the head apart!

In any case, "turning away from the polytheists" here either means indifference or ceasing to engage in combat and battle with them, because at that time, the power of the Muslims had not yet reached the stage where they could engage in armed struggle against the enemy's violence.

[^1]: For further explanation, refer to Volume 6, page 88 of Tafsir Nemouneh.

Then, to strengthen the heart of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, Allah reassures him that He will protect him against the mockers, saying: "Indeed, We are sufficient for you against the mockers" (إِنَّا كَفَيْناكَ الْمُسْتَهْزِئِينَ‌).

The mention of this sentence in the "past tense" (فعل ماضى), despite it referring to the future, apparently indicates the certainty of this protection. That is, we will certainly repel their evil from you, and this is a settled and finished matter.

Of course, some commentators have narrated a tradition that six groups (or fewer), each in their own way, used to mock the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Whenever he rose to call people to Islam, they would try with their words to disperse people from around him. However, Allah afflicted each of them with a calamity, and they became so preoccupied with themselves that they forgot the Prophet (the description of their afflictions is mentioned in some commentaries).

Then He describes the "mockers" as follows: "Those who set up another deity with Allah; but they will soon come to know [the consequences]" (الَّذِينَ يَجْعَلُونَ مَعَ اللَّهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ‌). This expression may be an indication that these are people whose very thoughts and actions are ridiculous, for they are so ignorant that they have carved a deity out of stone and wood in the face of Allah, the Creator of the universe—yet they wish to mock you!

Once again, as a consolation and to further strengthen the spirit of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He adds: "And We already know that your breast is constrained by what they say" (وَ لَقَد نَعْلَمُ أَنَّكَ يَضِيقُ صَدْرُكَ بِما يَقُولُونَ‌).

Your refined soul and sensitive heart cannot endure all this slander and words of disbelief and polytheism, and for this reason, you become distressed.

But do not be distressed; to erase the effects of their ugly and improper words, "Celebrate the praise of your Lord and be among those who prostrate" (فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَ كُنْ مِنَ السَّاجِدِينَ‌)! For this glorification of Allah, firstly, erases the bad effects of their speech from the hearts of those who yearn for Allah, and beyond that, it grants you strength and ability, gives light and purity, creates brightness and polish, strengthens your bond with God, makes your will powerful, and grants you greater endurance, more persistent struggle, and firmer steps. Hence, we read in narrations from Ibn Abbas: Whenever the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله became saddened, he would rise for prayer and "wash away the effects of this grief and sorrow from his heart through prayer."

And finally, He gives him the last instruction in this regard: do not cease the worship of your Lord throughout your entire life, "And worship your Lord until the certainty (death) comes to you" (وَ اعْبُدْ رَبَّكَ حَتَّى يَأْتِيَكَ الْيَقِينُ‌). The well-known and famous view among commentators is that "certainty" (yaqin) here means death. Death is called certainty because it is a definitive matter; man may doubt anything, but he cannot harbor doubt regarding death.

Alternatively, it is because at the time of death, the veils are removed and realities become manifest before man's eyes, and a state of certainty is attained for him. In Surah al-Muddaththir, verses 46 and 47, we read from the words of the people of Hell: "And we used to deny the Day of Recompense, until there came to us the certainty (death)" (وَ كُنَّا نُكَذِّبُ بِيَوْمِ الدِّينِ حَتَّى أَتانَا الْيَقِينُ‌).

From this, it becomes clear that what is narrated from some Sufis—who have used the above verse as a pretext to abandon worship, saying: "The verse says: worship until certainty arrives, therefore after attaining certainty there is no need for worship!"—is a baseless and unfounded statement. This is because, firstly: by the testimony of some Quranic verses mentioned above, certainty means death, which will come to everyone, both the believers and the people of Hell.

Secondly: the addressee of this speech is the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the Prophet's station of certainty is established for everyone. Can anyone claim that he did not possess the station of certainty in terms of faith?

Thirdly: successive (mutawatir) histories show that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did not abandon worship until the final hours of his life, and Ali عليه السلام was killed in the mihrab of worship, as were the other Imams علیهم السلام.

Key Points

1- The Beginning of the Public Call of Islam

As inferred from some narrations, the verses "Proclaim openly what you are commanded and turn away from the polytheists; indeed, We are sufficient for you against the mockers" were revealed in Mecca after the Prophet of Islam had called people secretly for three years, and a small number of his close associates had believed in him—the first of whom among women was Khadija and among men was Ali عليه السلام.

Naturally, calling toward pure Monotheism and dismantling the system of polytheism and idol-worship at that time and in that environment was a strange and terrifying task. For this reason, from the very beginning, it was apparent that a group would rise in mockery. Therefore, Allah grants strength of heart to His Prophet so that he does not fear the multitude of enemies and mockers, and makes his call open and public, engaging in a persistent logical struggle in this path [^1].

2- The Effect of Turning Toward God on Spiritual Power

In human life, there have always been and will be problems; this is the nature of worldly life. The greater a person is, the greater these problems are. From this, one can realize the magnitude of the problems the Prophet faced in that great mission.

However, we see that Allah commands His Prophet that in order to gain more strength and expansion of the breast (su'at al-sadr) against the multitude of these problems, he should turn to the glorification of his Lord, supplication, and prostration at His threshold. This shows that worship has a profound effect on strengthening the spirit, faith, and will of man. It is also understood from various narrations that the great leaders, when faced with crises and immense problems, would go to the door of God's house and find peace and strength in the light of His worship.

3- Worship and Perfection

[Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 11; p. 144]

We know that man is a being with the highest potential for perfection; he began his movement from the point of non-existence and continues toward infinity, and never

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Volume 3, page 32.

will the wheel of his perfection (as long as he is on the path) stop.

On the other hand, we know that worship is a high school of education; it awakens human thought, directs his mind toward the Infinite, washes the dust of sin and negligence from the heart and soul, nurtures high human qualities within him, strengthens the spirit of faith, and grants man awareness and responsibility.

For this reason, it is impossible for man to be without need of this great educational school for even a moment in life. Those who think that man can reach a point where he has no need for worship have either perceived human perfection as limited or have not understood the concept of worship.

Allamah Tabataba'i in "Tafsir al-Mizan" has a statement in this regard, the summary of which we provide below: "All beings in this world move toward perfection, and the perfection of the human species takes place within society; for this reason, he was created social by nature.

On the other hand, society can guarantee the nurturing and perfection of man only if it possesses organized rules and laws, and the individuals of the society, in the light of respecting those laws, manage their affairs, prevent collisions, and specify the limits of responsibilities.

In other words, if human society becomes righteous, individuals can reach their ultimate goal within it, and if it becomes corrupt, individuals are held back from this perfection. These rules and laws, whether social or devotional (ibadi), will be effective only if they are taken through the path of Prophethood and heavenly revelation.

We also know that devotional rules constitute a part of this individual and social perfection. From here it becomes clear: as long as human society stands and his life continues in this world, divine obligations will also continue. The dismantling of the apparatus of obligation (taklif) for man means forgetting the rules and laws, and its result is the corruption of human society!

It is also noteworthy that righteous deeds and acts of worship are the source of attaining virtuous spiritual habits (malakat). When these deeds are performed sufficiently and those virtuous habits become strong in the human soul, those habits will, in turn, become the source of more righteous deeds and obedience and servitude to God.

From here it becomes clear that those who have imagined that the goal of obligation is the completion of man—and therefore, when man reaches his perfection, the continuation of obligation has no meaning—is nothing more than a fallacy. For if man stops performing obligations, society will immediately move toward corruption, and in such a society, how can a perfect individual live? And if, despite having virtuous habits, he stops the worship and servitude of God, it means these habits have failed to produce their definitive effects (reflect on this) [^1]."

End of Surah al-Hijr

[^1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Volume 12, page 210.
